text: BACHELOR OF SCIENCE IN BEHAVIORAL SCIENCES & PREDICTIVE ANALYTICS hss@iitjammu.ac.in 1800 180 7101 Table of Contents The Overview of the BS Program 03 Highlights of the BS Program 07 Career Prospects 10 Admission Process 15 Academic Departments and Centers at IIT Jammu 17 The Department of Humanities and Social Sciences 19 Student Life and Campus Facilities 23 Contact Information 29 IIT JAMMU Overview of the BS PROGRAM Bachelor of Science (BS) in Behavioral Sciences and Predictive Analytics (BSPA) The Bachelor of Science in Behavioral Sciences and Predictive Analytics (BSPA) is inherently interdisciplinary, merging two distinct but complementary fields: Behavioral sciences and predictive analytics. The program integrates the study of human Behavior with advanced data analysis techniques, providing students with a holistic understanding of both qualitative and quantitative dimensions of human actions and decisions. This unique combination addresses the need for professionals who can interpret human Behavior using data-driven approaches, making it relevant across various sectors such as healthcare, public policy, marketing, and social sciences. The program's interdisciplinary nature equips students with the tools to analyze complex social issues using Behavioral insights and predictive models. The dual focus on theoretical understanding and practical application enables students to bridge the gap between humanities and data science, preparing them for a wide range of careers where both critical thinking and technical expertise are essential. In summary, BSBSPA’s curriculum draws from multiple academic disciplines, ensuring that graduates are well-rounded professionals capable of addressing complex societal challenges through innovative, interdisciplinary methods. Students will engage in rigorous research methodologies and learn to apply predictive models to real-world scenarios. This dual focus prepares graduates to contribute to academic research and to provide actionable insights in practical settings, bridging the gap between theory and practice. 3 Who We Are IIT Jammu, established in 2016, has navigated a remarkable journey from Paloura to Jagti, overcoming significant challenges. Our progress can be distilled into a single word: "unwavering." Like a tree that grows stronger with time, our institute has advanced with determination, driven by our faculty's pursuit of innovation and excellence. Located on a sprawling 400-acre permanent campus in Jagti, Nagrota, the institute offers several B.Tech, M.Tech, MS, and PhD programs with addition of new courses in 2025–BTech in Engineering Physics and Bachelors in Behavioral Science and Predictive Analytics. We've forged substantial international collaborations with esteemed institutions, including National Chung Cheng University, the University of Bergen, and the University of Alabama, among others. Our faculty has consistently attracted prestigious research investments, contributing to our expanding research landscape. Notably, 14 faculty members are supported under the SAP program, and eight under the SIRE program. Our research endeavors have yielded impressive results. The Biosciences and Bioengineering Department secured Rs. 1.67 crore in grants, paving the way for a Bio-Interface Technology Master's program. We've also seen significant growth in externally funded projects, with 74 projects worth nearly Rs. 20 crore added last year, bringing our total to 194 projects. Furthermore, we've collaborated on 84 consultancies, with a sanctioned amount exceeding Rs. 5 crore. Our Central Instrumentation Facility (CIF), or Saptarishi, has become a hub for pioneering research, with seven state-of-the-art labs. The Lithography and Fabrication lab is operational, and the Raman Spectroscopy equipment is actively used in the Spectroscopy lab. Building on this momentum, in 2024, we continued to improve our NIRF ranking in the Engineering Category. Our research and consultancy efforts have yielded remarkable results, with 103 sanctioned research projects between April 2023 and September 2024, totaling over Rs. 50 crore. We're proud to lead a

(CIF), or Saptarishi, has become a hub for pioneering research, with seven state-of-the-art labs. The Lithography and Fabrication lab is operational, and the Raman Spectroscopy equipment is actively used in the Spectroscopy lab. Building on this momentum, in 2024, we continued to improve our NIRF ranking in the Engineering Category. Our research and consultancy efforts have yielded remarkable results, with 103 sanctioned research projects between April 2023 and September 2024, totaling over Rs. 50 crore. We're proud to lead a consortia-based mechanism for the Central Project Management Unit for AI, funded by the Ministry of Education with a financial outlay of around Rs. 1000 crore. Our students have also excelled, with 70.8% of the batch of 2024 securing coveted campus offers despite the industry recession era. Through these achievements, IIT Jammu reaffirms its commitment to innovation, research, and student success. With a growing reputation in innovation, startups, and social impact projects in J&K, IIT Jammu is cementing its place among India’s leading engineering institutions. 4 Message from Department of Humanities and Social Sciences It gives us great pleasure to introduce the Bachelor of Science in Behavioral Sciences and Predictive Analytics, an innovative undergraduate program crafted by the Department of Humanities and Social Sciences at IIT Jammu. This program brings together core ideas from psychology, cognitive science, Behavioral economics, and sociology, and integrates them with statistical reasoning, data science, and predictive modelling. Our goal is to help students understand human behavior not just at the level of individual experience, but also as complex, measurable patterns that unfold across social systems and environments.This interdisciplinary program is designed to bridge the understanding of human behavior with data-driven decision-making. By integrating insights from behavioral sciences, psychology, sociology, and economics with tools from statistics, data analysis, and computational modelling, students will be uniquely equipped to decode complex human patterns and contribute to solutions in domains ranging from mental health and education to policy, design, and technology. At the Department of Humanities and Social Science, we are committed to fostering analytical thinkers and socially conscious individuals. This program is a reflection of our vision—to prepare students who are not only adept in behavioral theory but also skilled in the application of predictive tools to address real-world challenges. We invite curious minds to embark on this exciting academic journey and become a part of a future-ready generation of behavioral scientists and analysts. Warm regards, Department of Humanities

This program seamlessly combines the study of human Behavior with advanced analytical techniques, offering a unique interdisciplinary education that is essential in today’s data-driven world. Addressing Complex Social Issues By integrating behavioral sciences with predictive analytics, this program equips students with the skills to understand and predict human behavior in various contexts. This is crucial for addressing complex social issues such as mental health, social inequality, consumer behavior, and public policy. Career Prospects IIT JAMMU Graduates of the BSBSPA program are well-equipped for diverse career opportunities that blend human behavioral understanding with advanced data analysis. With expertise in both fields, they are uniquely positioned for roles across multiple industries, where data-driven decision-making and behavioral insights are increasingly crucial. The combination of behavioral theory and predictive analytics ensures that BSBSPA graduates have a broad skill set, making them versatile and competitive in the job market. Their ability to provide insights into human behavior using data-driven tools makes them valuable across sectors. Public Health and Healthcare Roles: Health Data Analyst, Behavioral Scientist, Epidemiologist, Health Policy Analyst Prospects: Graduates can contribute to designing public health interventions, predicting disease outbreaks, and analyzing health-related behaviors to improve patient outcomes. Marketing and Consumer Insights Roles: Market Research Analyst, Consumer Behavior Specialist, Data-Driven Marketing Manager Prospects: Understanding and predicting consumer behavior helps businesses improve products, personalize marketing strategies, and forecast market trends. 11 IIT JAMMU Policy Making and Governance Roles: Policy Analyst, Behavioral Insights Consultant, Public Policy Advisor Prospects: Graduates can work in government or think tanks to use behavioral data and predictive models to shape effective public policies on issues like mental health, education, and inequality. Human Resources and Organizational Behavior Roles: HR Data Analyst, Organizational Development Consultant, Talent Analytics Specialist Prospects: Predictive analytics can be used to assess employee engagement, predict turnover, and improve workplace culture and productivity. Education and EdTech Roles: Learning Analyst, Educational Psychologist, Instructional Designer Prospects: Graduates can develop predictive models for student performance, personalize learning experiences, and optimize educational programs using behavioral data. 12 IIT JAMMU Technology and User Experience Roles: UX Researcher, Data Scientist, Product Manager Prospects: Graduates can apply their skills to improve user experience, design data-driven products, and predict user behavior in app development and web design. Finance and Risk Management Roles: Behavioral Economist, Risk Analyst, Financial Data Scientist Prospects: Behavioral analytics is increasingly used in finance to understand investor behaviour, reduce risks, and predict market movements. Consulting and Advisory Roles Roles: Management Consultant, Behavioral Science Consultant, Data-Driven Strategy Consultant Prospects: Consulting firms seek experts who can analyze complex datasets, draw behavioral insights, and provide strategic guidance to businesses and governments. 13 IIT JAMMU Research and Academia Roles: Academic Researcher, Data Scientist in Behavioral Research, University Lecturer Prospects: Graduates may pursue advanced research or teaching careers in behavioral sciences and predictive analytics, contributing to academic knowledge

Facilities at IIT Jammu IIT JAMMU Academic Clubs IIT Jammu fosters holistic student development through a range of vibrant academic clubs that go beyond the traditional curriculum. These interdisciplinary clubs—such as the Coding Club, Robotics Club, FinTech/Popular Talk Club, Mesh, SAE-Baja Club, RE4M (Research for Mind), and E-Cell—offer students valuable exposure to various scientific and technical fields. By engaging in hands-on projects, workshops led by industry experts, and student-led competitions and events, members sharpen their practical skills and gain the confidence to address real-world challenges. Supported by dedicated faculty and well- equipped resources, these clubs provide a dynamic platform for collaboration, innovation, and leadership, significantly enriching both academic and professional journeys. Cultural Clubs IIT Jammu nurtures a vibrant cultural ecosystem through a variety of clubs that offer a refreshing counterbalance to its strong focus on science and technology. These clubs spanning dance, music, drama, art, literature, photography, and filmmaking, serve as platforms for creative expression and personal development. Valued on par with academic endeavors, they encourage students to explore their passions, build confidence, and grow as individuals. Clubs such as the Photography and Filmmaking Club, Dance Club, Music Club, Literary Club, Fine Arts Club, and Drama Club regularly host events, competitions, and performances that inspire students to push their boundaries. Enriching workshops and speaker sessions further complement these activities, fostering a dynamic and expressive campus culture. 24 IIT JAMMU Social Clubs IIT Jammu hosts several socially driven clubs—such as Kritash: Let’s Create Hope, WYoM, Ek Bharat Shreshtha Bharat (EBSB), and the Nature and Adventure Club (NAC)—that inspire students to look beyond academics and engage meaningfully with the world around them. These clubs encourage active citizenship, compassion, and a commitment to social impact. Kritash enables students to contribute to nearby villages through educational and social initiatives. EBSB offers unique opportunities for cultural exchange and travel, allowing students to experience the rich diversity of India. NAC caters to nature lovers, combining adventure with ecological awareness and exploration. WYoM nurtures a deeper connection to spirituality and inner well-being, supporting students in their journey of holistic development. Anhad Anhad: The Realm of Limitlessness is IIT Jammu’s flagship annual techno-cultural fest and the largest of its kind in Jammu & Kashmir. Attracting students from across the region, Anhad offers a vibrant blend of experiences—ranging from cutting-edge technical competitions like Intmaestro to high- energy cultural celebrations such as DJ nights and prom evenings. Spanning five dynamic

with ecological awareness and exploration. WYoM nurtures a deeper connection to spirituality and inner well-being, supporting students in their journey of holistic development. Anhad Anhad: The Realm of Limitlessness is IIT Jammu’s flagship annual techno-cultural fest and the largest of its kind in Jammu & Kashmir. Attracting students from across the region, Anhad offers a vibrant blend of experiences—ranging from cutting-edge technical competitions like Intmaestro to high- energy cultural celebrations such as DJ nights and prom evenings. Spanning five dynamic days, the fest provides a perfect platform for students to connect, showcase their talents, and create unforgettable memories in an atmosphere of creativity, collaboration, and celebration. 25 IIT JAMMU The Sports Fest (Pravaah) The annual inter-branch sports competition is a spirited tradition at IIT Jammu, offering freshers a great way to bond with seniors. As the campus turns into a competitive arena, students give their all through cheers, challenges, and fierce gameplay to win glory for their departments. From cricket and basketball to chess and pool, a variety of games contribute to each branch’s score. Beyond the thrill of competition, the weeklong event fosters discipline, teamwork, resilience, and sportsmanship, teaching students to embrace both victory and defeat with grace. Hostels and Mess Halls Hostel life at IIT Jammu is a vital part of the student experience, offering more than just accommodation. It fosters independence, self-reliance, and lifelong friendships. The Jagti Campus hosts four main hostels, along with one satellite hostel, all equipped with automatic washing machines and 24/7 Wi-Fi. Rooms are available in both single and double occupancy, furnished with essential amenities including shelves and study space. IIT Jammu has four spacious and well-ventilated mess halls located in the Egret, Dedhar, Fulgar, and Canary hostels. They serve breakfast, lunch, dinner, and snacks daily. The Mess Committee curates a diverse menu featuring dishes from across India, while the Mess Council oversees daily operations. Regular inspections ensure food quality and smooth functioning. 26 IIT JAMMU Medical Facilities IIT Jammu’s medical center, located in the Faculty Quarters, offers 24x7 care with OPD, a 10-bed facility, BLS ambulance, and pharmacy. Specialist visits are regularly arranged. For advanced care, patients are referred to nearby hospitals. Student health is supported through medical insurance and accident coverage. text: # Page Title: Humanities and Social Sciences | IIT Jammu

## A Cultural Confluence: Celebrating Traditional Art of Jammu at IIT Jammu

Jammu, [09.02.2024] —Center of Essential Skills and Zuban-e-Kalam HSS, IIT Jammu in
collaboration with The Kala Kendra Society, Jammu, successfully inaugurated a cultural
event, "Understanding Traditional Art of Jammu," a vibrant celebration aimed
at exploring and honoring the rich cultural heritage of the Jammu region. This unique
gathering was graced by the presence of Prof. Manoj Singh Gaur, IIT Jammu, including
esteemed Chief Guest, Sh. Suresh Kumar Gupta, IFS, Principal Secretary to the
Government, Department of Culture, and Dr. Javaid Rahi, Chief Editor, Gojri Department
and Secretary of Kala Kendra, among others.

The event was set in motion with a heartfelt welcome by Dr. Quleen kaur Bijral, faculty
and coordinator, Zuban-e-Kalam HSS, IIT Jammu in which she highlighted the importance of
cultural interactions to boost the wholesome development of a student from academics,
research, and re-discovery of regional roots and identity through art. Dr. Amitash Ojha,
faculty and coordinator of CES highlighted the need for such activities designed to
spotlight the traditional art forms that form the essence of Jammu's cultural
identity. Dr. Rahi Ji introduced, the resource person of the event, Ms.Medhavi Sharma,
setting the stage for her to share insights into her creative journey and the
inspiration behind her art.

Ms. Medhavi Sharma is a distinguished artist hailing from the quaint town of Udhampur in
Jammu and Kashmir. Through her initiative called ‘Parolaa’, she has aimed to
explore and revive the rich local culture of the Jammu region and give it a strong
visual vocabulary. Ms. Sharma's lecture and exhibition provided attendees with an
immersive experience of the timeless appeal of local art forms, enriching their
understanding and appreciation of Jammu's cultural legacy of Basholi art and Duggar
culture.

The proceedings included a floral welcome of Sh. Suresh Kumar Gupta by the Director of
IIT Jammu, followed by felicitations of the dignitaries in the presence of Prof. Bijoy
H. Boruah, senior faculty at HSS, IIT Jammu. The event further engaged the audience
through a Q&A session moderated by Sushmendra Kuril, BTech, Materials Engg offering
an interactive platform for discussions on traditional art and its contemporary
relevance.

The event concluded with a vote of thanks by Majid Majeed, CIO at Nucleon I3C, IIT JAMMU
who expressed gratitude to all participants, guests, and organizers. The gathering was
then invited for a group photo, symbolizing the unity and shared purpose among all
present, followed by a high tea, allowing for further conversations and networking.

# Page Title: Humanities and Social Sciences | IIT Jammu

### About Us

Welcome to the Humanities and Social Sciences Department of IIT Jammu! We are a group of dynamic, dedicated and inquisitive faculty members and scholars, with diverse interests ranging from Philosophy to Literatures in English to Economics to Sociology, which is a testament to the vast potential of the Humanities and Social Sciences. We are committed to upholding an intellectually stimulating and inclusive environment where students and faculty engage with some of the most profound questions concerning human existence, psychology, society, economy, culture, and history. Our research queries and pursuits are in line with the larger vision of the Institute: to enable collaborative interventions that are humanistic, creative, and have a social impact.

The Department has developed a first-of-its-kind B.Sc. in Behavioural Science and Predictive Analytics, offering its students interdisciplinary expertise. The Department also offers a series of core and elective courses for the BTech students of the Institute, inculcating a spirit of curiosity and ethical considerations in budding engineers and scientists.

The Department has a steadily growing cohort of PhD students engaged in cutting-edge research, rigorously working towards broadening the scope of critical enquiry and innovative thinking within the ever-more overlapping and interdisciplinary domains of the humanities and social sciences. An Experiential Language Learning Lab to aid undergraduate students in honing their Language and Communication skills has been developed. The Department fosters a space for scientific enquiry and exchange of ideas through its workshops, conferences, cultural events and programs, apart from propagating discussions through its growing collections of books and other reading resources.

## Assessing Financial Inclusion and its Impact on Financial Literacy and Living Standards in Jammu & Kashmir and Ladakh

- Principal Investigator - Dr. Shafkat Shafi Dar
- Co  Principal  Investigator   - Prof Meenakshi Rajeev

This Ongoing Project examines the status of Financial inclusion in the state of Jammu and Kashmir as well as Ladakh through a filled survey.

It investigates how financial inclusion impacts livelihood and earnings

WINTER SCHOOL Advanced 12-17 Qualitative December Tools and 2022 Techniques in Humanities and Social Sciences Organised by : IIT Jammu Department of Humanities and Social Sciences Sponsored by : ICSSR Indian Council of Social Science Research and CeP, IIT Jammu Convener Dr. Sayantan Mandal i Faculty, HSS, IIT Jammu Advanced WINTER SCHOOL Qualitative and WORKSHOP Tools and 12-17 December 2022 Techniques Venue: IIT Jammu (in-person/ offline) in Humanities Sponsored by: ICSSR and CeP - IIT Jammu and Social Organised by: HSS – IIT Jammu Convenor: Dr. Sayantan Mandal Sciences The Department of Humanities and Social Sciences (HSS), IIT Jammu with the support from the Indian Council of Social Science Research (ICSSR), New Delhi and the Continuing Education Program (CeP), IIT Jammu, is organizing a week-long Winter School and Workshop on ‘Advanced Qualitative Tools and Techniques in Humanities and Social Sciences’ under the aegis of ‘Azadi Ka Amrit Mahotsav’ (Government of India). The Event is designed for the early stage researchers from all across the country. The Workshop/ Winter School plans to engage the scholars through interactive sessions by the experts / resource persons from interdisciplinary backgrounds belonging to various eminent Higher Education Institutions. However, recent times saw a growing body of qualitative research employing more empirically sound methods for its validation, including some interdisciplinary techniques. It creates a need to sensitize and enrich the PhD research scholars to the latest trends in this area, juxtaposed with the hands-on sessions. In alignment with the ethos of the National Education Policy (2020), the proposed workshop could go a long way in enhancing the research output from the HSS scholars. In this light, the Department of Humanities and Social Sciences (HSS), IIT Jammu is organising a week-long winter school and workshop to engage the PhD research students from diverse HSS backgrounds in enriching sessions by resource persons from interdisciplinary backgrounds. In this light, the Department of Humanities and Social Sciences (HSS), IIT Jammu is organising a week-long winter school and workshop to engage the PhD research students from diverse HSS backgrounds in enriching sessions by resource persons from interdisciplinary backgrounds. The workshop would broadly revolve around the fundamentals of qualitative research, academic writing and planning of the research, Literature review and its novel approaches, including meta-analysis, systematic literature review, and so on, research ethics, and finding appropriate journals. It would also include dedicated hands-on sessions for content analysis, scientometric analysis, referencing, and plagiarism check using appropriate software. Objectives  To enable the participants to get a deeper understanding of basic and advanced qualitative research methods.  To help PhD students pre-plan and manage their work.  To acquaint the participants with a comprehensive understanding of the literature review and its allied concepts.  To conduct hands-on training in selected software, including NVIVO, for disseminating knowledge on some advanced qualitative methods.  To impart knowledge for Scenarios on Qualitative Research, Scientometric analysis, etc.  To enable the participants to understand and appreciate the ethics in research. The advancements in qualitative research methodologies hold the potential to build a nuanced understanding of the research problems. The advanced methods in qualitative research will help the microscale strategy formulation in a scientific manner. The present workshop, through intensive sessions on advanced tools and techniques in qualitative research methods, expects the participants to ameliorate their research skills for effective analysis of literature, including the policies. A technically sound and robust analysis may also help them in underscoring the lacunas in the present policies from different spheres, paving the way for its redressal. 1 Experts’ Profile and Session Abstracts Prof. R. Title of The session: Fundamentals of Research Abstract: As a practising scientist for the past four decades, I have come to long believe that if ever there was a great job on this planet, it is that of a researcher. For it gives you a licence to pursue what you enjoy most, unfettered by the outcome. But my career has also taught me that as I enjoy these privileges, I need to follow some basic rules of the game, just as you would, if you were to enjoy driving your car or playing a game of chess. In this session, we shall ask ourselves three or four questions about research that hopefully you will find interesting as well as useful in navigating further into the workshop. First: what is the question that you are chasing to answer or address ? Why is it important or interesting to you ? Second: how do you propose to address the question that you think is interesting? Just as multiple paths can lead to your home from your office, how do you rationalize on the path that you would take to reach to an answer. Third: Having addressed the question, do you feel a sense of fulfilment, or is there a lurking fear that there may me more to the question than what you have answered or set out to answer ? Fourth: Research is a journey and not a destination. So when do we hang our boots and say, well it is time to move on, sufficiently aware that we might not have reached our goals ? The session will be conducted in a participative mode. So be ready to enjoy ! 2

text: today is mechanical or electrical/electronics. It is a combination of both usually with other disciplines like materials. Also Software is almost always a necessary part of most products. Interdisciplinary research is on the rise globally. Yet, several studies have shown that it often achieves lower impact compared to more specialized work, and is less likely to attract funding. Here, we seek to reconcile such evidence by analyzing 44,419 research grants awarded by the research councils in the UK. We find that researchers with an interdisciplinary funding track record dominate the network of academic collaborations, both in terms of centrality and knowledge brokerage, but such a competitive advantage does not translate into immediate return. Our results based on a matched pair analysis show that interdisciplinary researchers achieve lower impact with their publications in the short run; however, they eventually outperform their specialized counterparts in funding performance, both in terms of volume and value. These findings suggest that pursuing an interdisciplinary career may require perseverance to overcome extra challenges, but can pave the way for a more successful endeavor. 3 Prof. Bijoy Boruah, currently Visiting Professor at IIT Jammu, retired from IIT Delhi in 2018. His earlier teaching career began at IIT Kanpur. During his entire career, he spent one year as a Fellow of the Indian Institute of Advanced Study at Shimla, and as a Senior Fulbright Fellow for eight months in the University of Texas at Austin. His research interests include Philosophical Aesthetics, Philosophy of Mind, Philosophy of Literature, and Metaphysics of the Self. Prof. Bijoy Boruah IIT JAMMU Title of The session: Research Ethics in Social Science Research : The Normative Underpinnings of Social-Scientific Research Abstract: I shall talk about the presuppositions of research ethics in social sciences by alluding to the normative underpinnings of social science, in as much as I am wedded to the belief that “value-free” social science is a myth. Setting the argument for a normative social science (NSS) against the so-called positive social science (PSS), I shall explain how the Fact/Value dichotomy is inapplicable to the study and examination of social phenomena. Furthermore, I shall try to show, by adverting to a theoretical observable on the part of Amartya Sen, how there exists a normative element hidden in PSS. This critique of PSS, mounted through Sen’s argumentative analysis of the false objectivity of individual choice or preference attributed to the so-called “rational economic actor/agent,”

metadata.title: Assets Images Conferences-Workshops Detailed Brochure Hss Winter School 2022.Pdf

text: normative social science (NSS) against the so-called positive social science (PSS), I shall explain how the Fact/Value dichotomy is inapplicable to the study and examination of social phenomena. Furthermore, I shall try to show, by adverting to a theoretical observable on the part of Amartya Sen, how there exists a normative element hidden in PSS. This critique of PSS, mounted through Sen’s argumentative analysis of the false objectivity of individual choice or preference attributed to the so-called “rational economic actor/agent,” is articulated through the illustration of the phenomenon of impoverishment and deprivation vis-à-vis the individual actor/agent’s desire-fulfilment psychology. I shall highlight the research ethics of social science studies in terms of how such researches are ideologically motivated by the desire to shape and strengthen the democratic fabric of our society. Social research into community development, which is a primary developmental condition in a democratic world, must comply with the imperative of launching programmes that enjoy peoples’ overwhelming acceptance and their whole- hearted participation. It is this acceptance and participation in a community context that explains why ethnography as a research methodology is so central to the qualitative approach in social research. I shall end the discussion by elaborating the ethnomethodological imperative in terms of its “holistic” and “naturalistic” character. 4 Malish.C.M is an Assistant Professor at the Ashank Desai Centre for Policy Studies at the Indian Institute of Technology (IIT) Bombay. Title of The session: Fundamentals of Qualitative Research Abstract: The session’s goal is to provide learners with a comprehensive understanding of the foundations of qualitative research. The session begins with introducing the logic of reasoning - inductive, deductive and abductive forms. It follows a discussion on how qualitative research benefits from various forms of reasoning and engages with theory. Participants will be exposed to real-life research problems to understand the distinction between the case-oriented and variable-oriented approaches to research. After introducing the process of sampling, data collection, data storage, reflexivity and theory building, participants will be asked to engage in practical sessions. Activities in the practical session include developing research questions and defending the choice of the method. 5 Dr Anupam Pachauri is a policy researcher in education and works at the Centre for Policy Research in Higher Education (CPRHE) of the National Institute of Educational Planning and Administration (NIEPA). Title of The session: Literature Review in Social Science Research Abstract: Participants in this workshop spread over two sessions will get hands-on experience at exploring the stages in empirical social research where the review of the existing literature is required and the nature of questions that a researcher must ask in the review process. They will also gain experience on how to systematically search through literature, identify databases, develop protocols for selecting studies and appraise the quality of selected studies, extract metadata, organise literature data for review, conduct meta- analysis, and report findings. Participants will also be introduced to different types of literature reviews, difference between literature review, literature survey and how they are different from writing annotated bibliography. The participants are expected to bring along their written piece of literature review, which could be any of the following: a section of their published research article, research report, thesis or dissertation. 6 Dr. Ankit is currently on foreign deputation as a full-time visiting researcher at the School of Engineering, Newcastle University, United Kingdom. text: Development. Title of The session: Using Scenarios for Qualitative Research Abstract: Scenarios are hypothetical but plausible propositions, images or stories about how the future might unfold. Scenarios are systematically constructed to aid decision-making in the future. The use of scenarios as a research methodology was probably first introduced by Berger in 1964. Today, scenarios has developed into a sophisticated research approach characterized by a wide range of quantitative (modeling and computer simulations) and qualitative (interviews, brainstorming workshops, group discussions, surveys, Delphi-style ranking, role playing, storytelling, intuitive logic and visioning) approaches. This session focuses on the use of scenarios for qualitative research. Participants will be provided with readings in- advance. They are expected to read these before attending the proposed session. text: INTERNATIONAL CONFERENCE ROADMAP FOR HUMANITIES & SOCIAL SCIENCES IN STEM HIGHER EDUCATION MARCH 19 - 20, 2021 Higher education across the world has witnessed unprecedented expansion and transformation in the past decades. The focus on technology, employability and innovation have taken the front seats aiming at faster growth for individual, market and the society at large. Most countries have set up mechanisms to focus on Technical and Professional higher education, as these disciplines and subjects provide direct linkages with the world of work. As a consequence, the demand for such subjects skyrocketed and higher education institutions providing degrees and diplomas in technical and professional disciplines mushroomed all over. This emerging limelight on technical and professional subjects have changed many facets of higher education today and in the process, the importance of the traditional liberal arts subjects, particularly Humanities and Social Sciences (HSS) subjects seem shrinking. The HSS subjects such as Literature, Sociology, Philosophy, History and so on are no longer considered equally important for the development of critical thinking in the students going for higher education. However, the HSS subjects meant to broaden the learners’ intellectual horizons, force them to think critically, to debate, and to grapple with ambiguities inherent in subjects. They are also designed to help the learners cultivate empathy for others in subjects and are particularly designed to develop well-rounded human beings. At the same time, it is equally important for the Humanities and Social Science subjects to evolve, to change according to the demands of the society, especially with the metamorphosis of the technology, global economy, industries and world of work. Fortunately, the global and national level policies related to Higher Education are bringing their focus back to the HSS and making them an integral part of technical and professional higher education. For instance, the National Education Policy of India (NEP 2020) provides utmost importance on the Humanities and Social Sciences and focuses on multidisciplinary learning. The Policy recognizes that there will be a growing demand for humanities and social sciences across the sciences, in order to ensure the unity and integrity of all knowledge. Assessments of educational approaches in undergraduate education that integrate the humanities and arts with Science, Technology, Engineering and Mathematics (STEM) have consistently showed positive learning outcomes, including increased creativity and innovation, critical thinking and higher-order thinking capacities, problem-solving abilities, teamwork, communication skills, more in depth learning and mastery of curricula across

text: The Policy recognizes that there will be a growing demand for humanities and social sciences across the sciences, in order to ensure the unity and integrity of all knowledge. Assessments of educational approaches in undergraduate education that integrate the humanities and arts with Science, Technology, Engineering and Mathematics (STEM) have consistently showed positive learning outcomes, including increased creativity and innovation, critical thinking and higher-order thinking capacities, problem-solving abilities, teamwork, communication skills, more in depth learning and mastery of curricula across fields, increases in social and moral awareness; besides general engagement and enjoyment of learning (NEP, 2020). To (re)establish Humanities and Social Sciences, it is necessary to invest in subject-specific and interdisciplinary research, innovative course and curriculum development, faculty orientation and so forth. The first step in this direction is to open the windows of dialogue and cross disciplinary knowledge sharing regarding various aspects of HSS subjects, innovative pedagogies for the progress and enlightened nature of a nation and its higher education learners, who are also the future leaders. Considering the need of the hour, the Department of Humanities and Social Sciences (HSS) of the Indian Institute of Technology Jammu (IIT Jammu) is going to organize an Online International Conference on the present and future of the Humanities and Social Sciences in Technical and Professional Higher Education on 19-20 March 2021. This Conference is an effort to bring together renowned academics, policy-makers and young researchers to engage in activities related to improving and integrating HSS with the technical and professional higher education, set roadmaps for its development and to learn from different experiences and practices. OBJECTIVES The major objectives of this proposed International Conference are: • To provide a platform for experts, researchers and practitioners to share their experiences on the present and future of HSS. • To discuss innovative practices in the area of HSS in Technical and Professional Higher Education for the improvement of the learners, the subjects and of the higher education sector(s). • To explore possibilities of promoting interdisciplinary research studies and, • Bring hi-quality academic publications (Edited Volume from an internationally recognized publishing house). In addition to the above, the Conference will create an excellent opportunity to form an international network of partners, researchers, experts and institutional leaders for sustainable knowledge-building. text: Ka Amrit Mahotsav Azadi Ka Amrit Mahotsav is an initiative of the Government of India to celebrate and commemorate 75 years of independence and the glorious history of it's people, culture and achievements. This Mahotsav is dedicated to the people of India who have not only been instrumental in bringing India thus far in its evolutionary journey but also hold within them the power and potential to enable Prime Minister Narendra Modi's vision of activating India 2.0, fuelled by the spirit of Aatmanirbhar Bharat. The official journey of Azadi Ka Amrit Mahotsav commenced on 12th March 2021 which started a 75-week countdown to our 75th anniversary of independence and will end post a year on 15th August 2023. Source: amritmahotsav.nic.in Indian Council of Social Science Research Indian Council of Social Science Research (ICSSR) was established in the year of 1969 by the Government of India to promote research in Social Sciences in the country. The Council Advise the Government of India on all matters pertaining to social science research as may be referred to it from time to time; and take such measures generally as may be necessary from time to time to promote social science research and its utilization. Source: ICSSR.org This Winter School and Workshop is jointly funded by ICSSR and CeP, IIT Jammu. vii Concept Note Contemporary research does not fail to bemoan the traditional qualitative research methods in Humanities and Social Sciences (HSS), often challenged on the grounds of bias and subjectivity. However, recent times saw a growing body of qualitative research employing more empirically sound methods for its validation, including some interdisciplinary techniques. It creates a need to sensitize and enrich the PhD research scholars to the latest trends in this area, juxtaposed with the hands-on sessions. The advancements in qualitative research methodologies hold the potential to build a nuanced understanding of the research problems. The advanced methods in qualitative research could help the microscale strategy formulation in a scientific manner. In this light, the Department of Humanities and Social Sciences (HSS), IIT Jammu, organized a week- long winter school and workshop to engage the PhD research students from diverse HSS backgrounds in enriching sessions by resource persons from interdisciplinary backgrounds. The workshop was organized at the main campus of IIT Jammu from 12-17 Dec 2022. It was jointly funded by ICSSR and CeP (Continuing Education Programme), IIT Jammu. In the HSS- IIT Jammu Winter

metadata.title: Assets Images Conferences-Workshops Report Hss Winter School 2022.Pdf

text: strategy formulation in a scientific manner. In this light, the Department of Humanities and Social Sciences (HSS), IIT Jammu, organized a week- long winter school and workshop to engage the PhD research students from diverse HSS backgrounds in enriching sessions by resource persons from interdisciplinary backgrounds. The workshop was organized at the main campus of IIT Jammu from 12-17 Dec 2022. It was jointly funded by ICSSR and CeP (Continuing Education Programme), IIT Jammu. In the HSS- IIT Jammu Winter School and Workshop 2022, various activities such as Academic Lectures, Special Sessions, Photo Competition and Exhibition were also integrated to celebrate Azadi ka Amrit Mahotsav. In alignment with the ethos of the National Education Policy (2020), the workshop went a long way in enhancing the research output of the HSS scholars. It broadly revolved around the fundamentals of qualitative research, academic writing and planning of the research, Literature review and its novel approaches, including meta-analysis, systematic literature review, and so on, research ethics, and finding appropriate journals. It also included dedicated hands-on sessions for content analysis, scientometric analysis, scenarios, referencing, and plagiarism checks using appropriate software. . Objectives  To enable the participants to get a deeper understanding of basic and advanced qualitative research methods.  To help PhD students pre-plan and manage their work.  To acquaint the participants with a comprehensive understanding of the literature review and its allied concepts.  To conduct hands-on training in selected software, including NVIVO, for disseminating knowledge on some advanced qualitative methods.  To impart knowledge for Scenarios on Qualitative Research, Scientometric analysis, etc.  To enable the participants to understand and appreciate the ethics in research. The present workshop, through intensive sessions on advanced tools and techniques in qualitative research methods, helped ameliorate the research skills of the participants for effective analysis of literature, including the policies. Skills developed during the workshop helped participants carry on with the technically sound and robust analysis, which further aided in underscoring the lacunas in the present policies from different spheres, paving the way for its redressal. 1 Contribution of the workshop to the existing body of Research The qualitative research methods face a pressing need to adopt advanced methods, which are technically sound. The existing body of research on Qualitative methods faces a two- fold threat: (i) intrinsic and (ii) extrinsic. The intrinsic threat arises from a lack of methods to validate the methodology,

metadata.title: Assets Images Conferences-Workshops Report Hss Winter School 2022.Pdf

text: robust analysis, which further aided in underscoring the lacunas in the present policies from different spheres, paving the way for its redressal. 1 Contribution of the workshop to the existing body of Research The qualitative research methods face a pressing need to adopt advanced methods, which are technically sound. The existing body of research on Qualitative methods faces a two- fold threat: (i) intrinsic and (ii) extrinsic. The intrinsic threat arises from a lack of methods to validate the methodology, like in the case of hard sciences. The extrinsic threat directly arises from the positivist paradigm of Quantitative methods. However, recent times have seen two paramount shifts, mentioned as follows: (i) refinement of the existing literature on qualitative methods by acknowledging the need to establish a common framework for various qualitative methods to enhance the trustworthiness and validity of the findings, and (ii) new methods developing in the qualitative field. The advancements in qualitative research methodologies hold the potential to build a nuanced understanding of the research problems. These findings can feed the policies by providing empirically grounded suggestions, and highlight the need for custom solutions. Also, the advanced methods in qualitative research could help the micro-scale strategy formulation in a scientific manner. The workshop contributed to the existing body of research by familiarizing the refinements and advancements in the qualitative research paradigm, coupled with the recent trends to the participants. The updated knowledge and hands-on training with interdisciplinary faculty members in lieu of the objectives of the NEP 2020 helped the participants improve research findings to analyse and solve social problems in a more legitimate manner. Also, the workshop provided the participants the ground to discuss and debate various critical concerns, which remain embedded with diverse qualitative methods. Finally, the workshop fostered the sense of sustainability through interdisciplinary interactions, and planned activities to celebrate Azadi ka Amrit Mahotsav. 2 Presenters and Topics Prof. Uma Shankar is a Senior Professor, Department of Bioscience and Bioengineering, IIT Jammu. He is an elected Fellow of the Indian Academy of Sciences, Bengaluru and a founder trustee of the Bangalore based Ashoka Trust for Research in Ecology and the Environment. He has been an honorary fellow of the Jawaharlal Nehru

He is an elected Fellow of the Indian Academy of Sciences, Bengaluru and a founder trustee of the Bangalore based Ashoka Trust for Research in Ecology and the Environment. He has been an honorary fellow of the Jawaharlal Nehru Center for Advanced Prof. Uma Scientific Research, Bengaluru and an honorary research associate at the University of New Brunswick, Canada, Shankar besides being a Fulbright and Visiting Professor at the University of Massachusetts, Boston. Basically trained as a IIT JAMMU geneticist, his interests has spanned multiple domains including plant evolutionary biology, conservation genetics, plant secondary metabolites and bioprospecting, plant microbiome and data mining. Title of The session: Fundamentals of Research Prof. Rakesh Singhai is currently working as Senior Professor in Mechanical Engineering department at IIT Jammu. He is also in charge of Institute Innovation and entrepreneurship council and also Continuing education and outreach activities. Prof. Singhai completed his B. Tech. in Mechanical Engineering from IIT Kanpur in 1978. text: Summary of the session Faculty In-Charge (FIC): Dr. Sayantan Mandal Rapporteur: Sheriya (HSS Scholar) Management & Logistics (M&L): Sumit (HSS Scholar) Prof. Uma Shankar succeeded in setting up the pulse of the participants by conducting a very critical and awakening warm-up session titled “Fundamentals of research” on 12th December 2022. The speaker was introduced by the Faculty in charge (FIC) of the session, Dr Sayantan Mandal. Further, Sheriya Sareen (Research Scholar) was the rapporteur for this session, while Sumit Sikdar (Research Scholar) was assigned the responsibility of Logistics and Management (L&M). Prof. Shankar, through multiple examples, inductively derived the working concept of “research” collectively with the participants, thereby ensuring high engagement and active participation of the participants. In the first example, he floated a very critical question to the participants: “Have you wondered why men are good at some tasks while women are not?” Through this example of polymorphism in gender, or simply gendered-task differentiation, he brought into light the typical stereotyping in our society. At the same time, he highlighted the work of Ragini Verma from the University of Pennsylvania, who studied the neural networks of male and female brains. She concluded that there are drastic differences between the neural connections of males and females: it’s hereditary! Despite a huge uproar amongst the readers when this paper was released, it could scientifically give sanction to females being better at multitasking than men. In the second example, he showed a picture where birds were making their nests from cigarette butts. He asked the participants to think of plausible reasons for the same. Thereafter, he explained the results of a study which found that the birds had somehow learned over time that ticks in the nest leave their babies morbid, and the nicotine in the cigarette butts repels the ticks, ensuring the safety of their babies. Third, he shared quite a quirky example where he asked participants about their preferences for Basmati rice and 12 then explained that the smell of the rice is the same as that of tigers’ urine due to the presence of the same chemical components. Consequently, he asked the participants what was common methodologically in the three examples. Based on the participants’ responses, he noted the steps as follows: (i) All three examples started with observations, (ii) Each example involved exploration through keywords like Why and How, (iii) Experiments were conducted in all cases to derive

metadata.title: Assets Images Conferences-Workshops Report Hss Winter School 2022.Pdf

text: Basmati rice and 12 then explained that the smell of the rice is the same as that of tigers’ urine due to the presence of the same chemical components. Consequently, he asked the participants what was common methodologically in the three examples. Based on the participants’ responses, he noted the steps as follows: (i) All three examples started with observations, (ii) Each example involved exploration through keywords like Why and How, (iii) Experiments were conducted in all cases to derive the results, and (iv) there was a sense of eureka in each of the above examples, due to the new knowledge or discovery. Based on these steps, he inductively derived the definition of Research in its simplest or practical form as something that is logically structured and self- constrained. He emphasised the need for the participants to find eureka in their research. We need to challenge ourselves to get the pleasure of experiencing our results. Thereafter, he re-defined the steps in the form of a flowchart, derived based on the participant’s responses to explicate the research cycle in a more structured way, as follows: Observations > Questions > Methods/ Experiments > Results/ Inference > Discovery/ New Knowledge Simultaneously, he also underscored that research is never a linear cycle. He underlined that the problem is if you do not want to travel from observations to questions because then you are not awakening yourself to the things around you. He advised the participants to be active observers and to pay utmost importance to the research questions. He also explained the importance of asking the right questions. He asked the participants how many of them framed their own research questions. Subsequently, he highlighted some practices in academia where the research questions are addressed by the scholar because (i) the supervisors want it to be answered or (ii) it is driven by a research call. Again, he suggested that the participants consider only those research questions in which they have an interest. It was followed by a hands-on session where Prof. Shankar gave some time to students to pen down the primary research question of their study and gave individual remarks to all the participants. Based on this discussion, he derived the anatomy of a good research question on the board, mentioned as follows: (i) Relevance (Importance and Implications), (ii) Clarity (Uncluttered), (iii) Answerable (Feasibility), (iv) Unique (Publishable), (v) Conceptualization/ Theorization, and (vi) Interesting.

The session ended with a discussion on the question which always gets divided opinions: Is hypotheses a liability in Social Sciences? 13 Session 2: Research Ethics in Social Science Research: The Normative Underpinnings of Social-Scientific Research by Prof. Bijoy Boruah Summary of the session Faculty In-Charge (FIC): Dr. Joby Varghese Rapporteur: Sumit (HSS Scholar) Management & Logistics (M&L): Ritesh (HSS Scholar) Prof. Boruah talked about the presuppositions of research ethics in social sciences by alluding to the normative underpinnings of social science, Setting the argument for a normative social science (NSS) against the so-called positive social science (PSS), the speaker explained how the Fact/Value dichotomy is inapplicable to the study and examination of social phenomena. He tried to show, by adverting to a theoretical observable on the part of Amartya Sen, how there exists a normative element hidden in PSS. This critique of PSS, mounted through Sen’s argumentative analysis of the false objectivity of individual choice or preference attributed to the so-called “rational economic actor/agent,” is articulated through the illustration of the phenomenon of impoverishment and deprivation vis-à-vis the individual actor/agent’s desire-fulfilment psychology. Prof. Boruah highlighted the research ethics of social science studies in terms of how such research are ideologically motivated by the desire to shape and strengthen the democratic fabric of our society. Social research into community development, which is a primary developmental condition in a democratic world, must comply with the imperative of launching programmes that enjoy peoples’ overwhelming acceptance and their whole- hearted participation. It is this acceptance and participation in a community context that explains why ethnography as a research methodology is so central to the qualitative approach in social research. He ended the discussion by elaborating the ethnomethodological imperative in terms of its “holistic” and “naturalistic” character. 14 Session 3: Special Session on Azadi Ka Amrit Mahotsav: National Education Policy 2020: Importance of Interdisciplinary

We need to consider the accidents at the same time. we need to focus on managerial bandwidth. We can identify the black spot and reduce many accidental deaths. With the resolution of behavioural issues, we can significantly reduce the number of accidents. Humanities research is very important for our social as well as technological development. Humanities is the foundation on which technological progress should be built. He concluded through his opinion that Huminites play an important role in the well-being of a nation. There were two segments to the session. The resource person started the session with a brief discussion on the principles of qualitative research in the Humanities and Social Sciences. He began by introducing the concept of qualitative research and how it allows for a deeper understanding of experiences, events, and context, as well as assisting researchers in finding answers to questions that cannot be quantified. He further stated that while developing transferable skills and abilities and adding to the body of information already known, doctoral education should not compromise on the ethical standards of social sciences research. Scholars need to uphold ethical standards while undertaking qualitative research projects that have human subjects. He went on to discuss the three basic types of logical reasoning, that is inductive, deductive, and abductive. This was followed by a discussion on the implications of various types of reasoning and how qualitative research interacts with these reasoning models. Further on, Dr Malish discussed philosophical terms such as ontology, epistemology and phenomenology. Participants were asked to partake in practical sessions in the second half of the session after receiving an overview of the sampling, data gathering, data storage, reflexivity, and theory development processes in the first half. This was done to make the session more engaging and help participants comprehend the differences between the case-oriented and variable- oriented methods of research. This practical session included exercises such as formulating research topics and taking a methodical approach to answering the research questions. Dr Malish concluded the session by addressing the concerns of participants regarding the publication process and publication ethics. The participants found the session extremely helpful in understanding the basics of research and various types of research methodologies. 16

The participants found the session extremely helpful in understanding the basics of research and various types of research methodologies. 16 Summary of Session 2: Fundamentals of Advanced Qualitative Research Methods II By Dr. Malish C M Faculty In-Charge (FIC): Prof. Bijou Boruah Rapporteur: Nadeem (HSS Scholar) Management & Logistics (M&L): Pummy (HSS Scholar) In his opening remarks, the speaker defined "qualitative" and "quantitative" research and outlined the main distinctions between the two methodologies. A quantitative research approach is often characterized as a "language of variables" where data is collected and analyzed in terms of measurable and quantifiable phenomena that can be manipulated, controlled, or measured in order to test a hypothesis or confirm or refute a theory. For example, how does poverty impact educational achievement among young adults? In this research question, the independent variable is poverty, and the dependent variable is educational attainment. The research may aim to explore the relationship between these two variables or to test the effect of poverty on educational achievement. The variable- oriented approach is primarily aimed at establishing generalized relationships between variables. On the other hand, a qualitative approach can be described as a "language of the case," in which data are collected and analyzed in relation to individuals, groups, events, or processes. A case can be an individual, an organization or a process. An example could be the coping mechanism in the research question, "How do physically challenged students cope with academic demands at a university?" Case-oriented research seeks to understand complex units or provide rich descriptions of phenomena. The speaker then discussed several key features of the qualitative approach, including: (i) The focus to develop or refine existing theories or to generate new theoretical insights, (b) The use of open-ended and flexible research designs that allow for the exploration of new or unexpected findings, (iii) Less possibility of division of labour because this approach generally requires researchers to be involved in all aspects of the research process, from data collection and analysis to interpretation and reporting, (iv) How it tries to

Day 4: 15.12.2022 Session 1: Literature Review in Social Science Research & Session by Dr. Anupam Pachauri Summary of Session 1 Faculty In-Charge (FIC): Dr. Sayantan Mandal Rapporteur: Aditi (HSS Scholar) Management & Logistics (M&L): Saloni (HSS Scholar) Dr Anupam Pachauri’s session focused on the nuances of literature review as part of the research process. She discussed various reasons for plagiarism while writing a paper, one reason being the laziness on part of the researcher which usually leads to the development 18 of poor paraphrasing. To make the session an interactive one, she divided the participants into five groups. For the first activity, the participants had to discuss the reasons for the concerns related to writing literature review. Each group discussed various concerns post the allotted time and came to the conclusion that literature review needs to be structured with a target in order to avoid the notion of it being a cumbersome activity. In this activity, group-1 raised the concern of identifying the research gaps in an area of research; group spoke about the problem of when to end a literature review, about how many research papers should be consulted; group-3 noted that it is pertinent to construct a valid argument and coherence is of utmost importance in a research article along with the need to look for the structure of the paper, thesis, data, etc; group-4 discussed about the parameters related to the existing literature in the area of research,

text: identifying the research gaps in an area of research; group spoke about the problem of when to end a literature review, about how many research papers should be consulted; group-3 noted that it is pertinent to construct a valid argument and coherence is of utmost importance in a research article along with the need to look for the structure of the paper, thesis, data, etc; group-4 discussed about the parameters related to the existing literature in the area of research, and the task of reading widely, and group-5 discussed about the main theme of the research paper. Dr Anupam discussed all these points raised by the participants and provided the following solutions-reading widely, taking notes, identifying sources, data set/prominent database to look at, with whom to speak, to whom approach-get in touch with the supervisor, consult the librarian, Google scholar, follow research organizations, become members of research groups. She further gave tips on how to review papers which are as follows, applying judgment on a text, and purposeful mindful reading to judge the article. She added that it is essential to look for the purposes of lit review, and find out what is covered by scholars in the field. Dr Anupam explained Cooper's taxonomy of Literature review(1988) and also addressed the common query of what makes a researcher a good researcher. Furthermore, the session ended with the unanimous decision of the participants and the instructor that there is not an absolute end to a literature review and that a researcher needs to find it on his/her own discretion. Group Presentations by the participants Participants are engaged in ‘Group discussion’, IIT Jammu 25 Day 6: 17.12.2022 Session I: Conducting Scientometrics and Bibliometrics Analysis: Overview and Application by Dr. Shiwangi Singh Summary of the session Faculty In-Charge (FIC): Dr. Joby Varghese Rapporteur: Ritesh (HSS Scholar) Management & Logistics (M&L): Aditi (HSS Scholar) Dr. Shiwangi delivered this very useful session on Bibliometrics analysis. She covered “How do we conduct bibliometrics analysis and also gave online hands-on experience in Scopus. It is very useful tool to analyse different research papers for research. When we cite the previous research in our work, we do Scientometric analysis. This is quantifiable phenomenon in scientific research. We talk about article, journals, references, citations etc. She explained bibliometric analysis as: (i) an overview of any particular topic, and (ii) a statistical method to analyse books, articles etc. The different methods of conducting Literature review include: (i) Systematic literature review, (ii) Meta- analysis,and (iii) Comparison of literature reviews. Meta Analysis is used when we want to empirically summarize the factors, sizable populations. We should have two different relationships when we do meta-analysis. Subjective biases are lower. Systematic literature review is used to analyse at least 40 articles or more. The Objective is to understand a particular construct from different perspective. With Bibliometric Analysis, we can analyse large number of papers; subjectivity biases are lower in this analysis. Dr. Shiwangi explained the reasons for using bibliometric analysis as: (i) Producing high research impact, (ii) when we have

metadata.title: Assets Images Conferences-Workshops Report Hss Winter School 2022.Pdf

text: empirically summarize the factors, sizable populations. We should have two different relationships when we do meta-analysis. Subjective biases are lower. Systematic literature review is used to analyse at least 40 articles or more. The Objective is to understand a particular construct from different perspective. With Bibliometric Analysis, we can analyse large number of papers; subjectivity biases are lower in this analysis. Dr. Shiwangi explained the reasons for using bibliometric analysis as: (i) Producing high research impact, (ii) when we have large number of scientific data, (iii) to uncover emerging trends in article and journal performance, and (iv) to identify Knowledge gaps. The Bibliometric analysis procedure broadly includes the following steps: (i) Define the aims and scope of the Bibiliometric study, (ii) Choose the techniques for bibliometric analysis, (iii) Collect the required data, and (iv) Perform the bibliometric analysis and report the findings. 26 To conduct this, some of the Frequently used databases include Web of science and Scopus. Further, some methods that can aid bibliometric analysis are: (i) Factor analysis, (ii) Regression analysis, and (iii) Systematic literature review. Dr Shiwangi towards the end also gave online hands-on experience to participants. It provided a comparative view of the topic to review and analyse different research literature. Session II: Session on Roadmap to Publication Summary of the session Panel Discussion: Title: Roadmap to publication Panellists: Dr Sanchayan Nath, Dr Shiwangi Singh, and Dr Sayantan Mandal Moderator: Sumit Sikdar Rapporteur and M&L: Sheriya (HSS Scholar) The Winter School and workshop included two-panel discussions. In the second panel discussion on “Roadmap to Publications”, moderated by Sumit Sikdar, the panellists were: Dr Sanchayan, Dr Shiwangi, and Dr Sayantan. The rapporteur for the session was Sheriya Sareen. The scope of questions covered both the technical and general aspects. Sumit floated a general question to the panellists, inquiring why the researchers should publish. While the need for both recognition and requirement was established, the discussion reiterated the following two-way process: one, the requirement to publish to help the emerging researchers get an understanding of what is already done and accordingly look for a researchable gap, and two, to make your own contribution in the pool of literature that already exists. Pertinent questions like, if not publication, then what? were also discussed. The session also included some technical questions, like how to find a suitable journal. The collated responses are mentioned below: one, it's always advisable to go

metadata.title: Assets Images Conferences-Workshops Report Hss Winter School 2022.Pdf

text: following two-way process: one, the requirement to publish to help the emerging researchers get an understanding of what is already done and accordingly look for a researchable gap, and two, to make your own contribution in the pool of literature that already exists. Pertinent questions like, if not publication, then what? were also discussed. The session also included some technical questions, like how to find a suitable journal. The collated responses are mentioned below: one, it's always advisable to go for keyword search in some prominent database like Scopus ad Web of Science; two, it is suggested to always look into the aims and scope of the shortlisted journals; third, it is preferred to have a list of back-up journals while sending your work to a specific one; fourth, it is always a good idea to see the reputation of the editorial board. It was emphasised that once you begin to develop a core understanding of your area, you become sensitised to the leading journals as well. Another pertinent aspect raised by Sumit was why HSS journals have less impact factor than Science journals. An important suggestion received in this context was to look for interdisciplinary journals. Also, it was suggested that journals with Impact Factor (IF) 2.0- 2.5 are considered reputable in HSS streams. The concept of the h-index, which is employed to measure the impact of journals, was also discussed. The next pertinent point of discussion was why scholarly journal gets more attention than periodicals. The reasons discussed were as follows: (i) certain periodicals may operate on some agendas, (ii) journals include the scope of addition of some relevant background academic literature, (iii) journals meet the specific requirements of researchers in a particular area, and (iv) journals are a reliable source to disseminate academic findings. Finally, the process of the blind peer review was discussed. Two major things were discussed here. The first was related to the right selection of journals. For instance, it is preferred to get your work on the LGBTQ community published in journals which are sensitive to its issues. It enhances the chances for your work to go into the hand of 27 appropriate reviewers, who can objectively and critically comment on your work. Second, it was emphasised that it is alright to decline a few comments that you get from the reviewers, given you have proper justification to defend your claims. The session

The session started with Sanchayan asking the groups to identify a unique thing about each other and thereafter, come up with a name for their group. He also asked each group to individually explain the rationale behind the group names. We saw a flurry of names, from the Dreamers to the Observers, to the Expressers, to the Dextrus, to the Spectrum, to the Social explorers, and so on. Building on the resources shared earlier, Sanchayan then posed a question to the participants about what, according to them, counts as interesting research. The following responses were noted: the one which gives new thoughts; the one which brings a aha- feel; the type of research which is beyond tweaking the existing research; the one that is 28 not bound by traditional ways. This paved way for the subsequent question on the emerging problems in research, for which the collated responses revolved around

metadata.title: Assets Images Conferences-Workshops Report Hss Winter School 2022.Pdf

text: the resources shared earlier, Sanchayan then posed a question to the participants about what, according to them, counts as interesting research. The following responses were noted: the one which gives new thoughts; the one which brings a aha- feel; the type of research which is beyond tweaking the existing research; the one that is 28 not bound by traditional ways. This paved way for the subsequent question on the emerging problems in research, for which the collated responses revolved around expenses, time-consuming, dearth of literature, difficulty in reaching out to different stakeholders, and publications challenge. Sanchayan then explains the concept of scenarios as plausible contexts, focusing on “what if.” Differentiating from focus-group discussions (FGDs), he explained that FGDs are a part of scenarios. Scenarios are developed over time, involving different stakeholders and including multiple improvements. While discussing this emerging concept, he also threw light on another new field of Qualitative Comparative Analysis (QCA). The application of scenarios was then discussed, which includes the following: (i) to reach an alternative for achieving the present mental model, (ii) to design scenarios to find the perspectives we normally do not find, (iii) to be prepared for crazy scenarios in the future. For instance, scenarios can be used to challenge the existing method of retailing in India, where the retail sector is still very unorganised. After the preliminary discussion, Sanchayan illustrated an example of using scenarios for qualitative research from his own practical experiences. He talked about his scenario-based approach to exfoliating how people who do not migrate continue living in Bangladesh, despite the rise in the sea level. Thereafter, he engaged the participants in an activity involving building scenario-based research, based on the Crawford’s framework, and grounded on the hypotheses: Communities can successfully solve collective action problems if they use resources and tactics for dealing with power imbalances and shocks. Participants sat in their allocated groups and brainstormed while Sanchayan went to each group to facilitate the discussion. Each group put up their ideas on chart papers and presented the scenarios designed by them based on the connecting broad research theme of the groups.
