text: LOCATION IIT JAMMU, JAGTI CAMPUS SUKANTA CHAUDHURI SUPRIYA CHAUDHURI Professor Emeritus Professor Emerita Jadavpur University Jadavpur University Contact sukany a.mondal@iitjammu.ac.in mehreenchoudhary59@gmail.com RITA KOTHARI RUKMINI BHAYA NAIR Professor of English Professor of English & Ashoka University Linguistics, IIT Delhi kaushikyhazra@gmail.com REGISTRATION LINK SOUJIT DAS Independent Art Historian & Curator ن भारतीय प्रौद्योगिकी DHEPARTMENoT OF संस्थान जम्मू INDIAN INSTITUTE OF HUMANITIES & TECHNOLOGY JAMMU SOCIAL SCIENCES विद्याधन सर्वधन प्रधानम् TEXT, PRE-TEXT, AND CONTEXT EXPLORING THE LAYERS OF LITERARY MEANING A 2-DAY WORKSHOP ORGANISED BY THE DEPARTMENT OF HUMANITIES AND SOCIAL SCIENCES IIT JAMMU speakers Our 7-8 NOVEMBER 2025 विद्याधन सबंधन प्रधानम् TEXT, PRE-TEXT, AND CONTEXT: EXPLORING THE LAYERS OF LITERARY MEANING A TWO-DAY WORKSHOP IN IIT JAMMU; 7-8 NOVEMBER 2025 ________________________________________________________________ What makes up the text, and how? Sukanta Chaudhuri, in his The Metaphysics of Text (2010), addresses this question in the spirit of a philosopher, suggesting that we investigate the ontology of a text “as a metaphysician investigates what makes up the apparent reality of our experience.” Bertrand Russell famously argued, a century ago, that the real physical object is a “logical construction” out of sense-data furnished by our sense experience. This was a metaphysical argument on a philosopher’s part, and a shocking one for the non-philosopher to hear that the real physical object, or the physically real object, is not an objectively given entity that we experientially encounter in the world we live, but is actually a “construction” out of something subjectively given to us in the mental space in the form of sense-data. Objective reality is a metaphysical construction out of subjective data. Is the “being” of a literary text likewise a constructed reality, a product of some properties of language or sign systems and other associated factors that are analogous to the metaphysician’s sense-data? Professor Chaudhuri’s project is a meta-textual enquiry, which amounts to engaging in a text that refers to and examines its own being. Is the text an abstract conceptual entity of signification, or does it also necessarily have a physical or material embodiment? Such a question makes the matter of textual identity—the core identity of a text—problematic. What happens to an oral text that subsequently becomes a written text, once the spoken words are inscribed on the pages of a book? Furthermore, what happens when that very text in the “book” form finds its new incarnation in the electronic medium, resulting in a digital text?

metadata.title: Assets Images News Text-Pre-Text-And-Context Text-Pre-Text-And-Context Brochure.Pdf

text: text an abstract conceptual entity of signification, or does it also necessarily have a physical or material embodiment? Such a question makes the matter of textual identity—the core identity of a text—problematic. What happens to an oral text that subsequently becomes a written text, once the spoken words are inscribed on the pages of a book? Furthermore, what happens when that very text in the “book” form finds its new incarnation in the electronic medium, resulting in a digital text? Does a single textual message undergo a process of clear transmission through multiple media, retaining its “original” content-identity? Professor Chaudhuri and other theories of contemporary and poststructuralist persuasions have tended to deconstruct the uncritical belief in the identity or being of a text as determined by its “original” meaning-content. Although these critiques have not gone so far as to say (with Marshal McLuhan) that “the medium is the message,” they have argued that the changing media make a substantive difference to the identity of the text. Professor Chaudhuri has strongly argued for the position that a text is more of a process than a finished product. Like the metaphysician’s “logical construction” theory of physical reality, Chaudhuri has also argued for a constructionist view and considered various forces of signification as complex constituting factors in the textual process. Given this constructionist view, the concept of textual identity across time would have to be modified into “changing textual identity,” necessitating a distinction between a work (authored originally by someone) and its multiple textual incarnations in the course of time. This distinction between work and text is therefore mapped onto the one-many relational nexus: one work “begetting” many texts in an endless process of signification. In this theoretical scenario, there is one work of which there could be many different texts in different periods of time, inscribed in different media, hence constituted in different material embodiments. The present workshop, Text, Pre-text, and Context, is an engagement with the indeterminate ontology of textuality, foregrounding the issue of continual change of media and material embodiment that inevitably reshapes the abstract, conceptual work of literary signification. The ontological indeterminacy is perhaps an inevitable consequence of the embeddedness of literary texts within an intricate network of pre-existing discourse, traditions, and interpretative frameworks. Modern literary theory has consistently drawn attention to the layered constitution of textual meaning. For example, questioning the original identity and authorial creativity of a

metadata.title: Assets Images News Text-Pre-Text-And-Context Text-Pre-Text-And-Context Brochure.Pdf

text: is an engagement with the indeterminate ontology of textuality, foregrounding the issue of continual change of media and material embodiment that inevitably reshapes the abstract, conceptual work of literary signification. The ontological indeterminacy is perhaps an inevitable consequence of the embeddedness of literary texts within an intricate network of pre-existing discourse, traditions, and interpretative frameworks. Modern literary theory has consistently drawn attention to the layered constitution of textual meaning. For example, questioning the original identity and authorial creativity of a work, Roland Barthes views the text as a “tissue of quotations” drawn from multiple other writings. Barthes’s “multidimensional space” of textual creation through mixing and blending diverse “quotations” is conceptualised by Julia Kristeva as “intertextuality,” the transposition of one sign system into another. Likewise, Hans-Georg Gadamer’s hermeneutics foregrounds the “historicity of understanding,” where both the text and the reader are situated within evolving horizons of meaning, leading to “fusion of horizons.” The workshop takes its central focus on the triadic relationship between text, pre-text, and context. Whilst text refers to a literary work as a structured, signifying artefact, pre-text encompasses sources, traditions, canonical antecedents, cultural archives, and discursive formations that condition the formation of the text. Context involves the historical, social, political, ideological, institutional, and other cultural frameworks within which texts are produced, circulated, and interpreted. By exploring the dynamic interplay of text, pre-text, and context in literary creation and interpretation, the workshop will examine how texts are shaped by preceding narratives, cultural frameworks, and interpretative environments. Attention will be focused on how meaning is constantly negotiated through this dynamic interplay of the different layers of this triad. By examining how meaning emerges at the intersection of these three domains, this workshop aims to deepen critical engagements with literature. It seeks to demonstrate that literary close reading is not just a formal exercise but an exploratory approach that is historically alert, intertextually aware, and theoretically informed. The two-day workshop is structured into different sessions of presentation of views by individual experts, ending with a panel discussion on the theme. Professor Chaudhuri has published extensively on the English and European Renaissance and edited many Renaissance texts, including the Third Arden edition of Shakespeare’s A Midsummer Night’s Dream. He has translated extensively into and out of Bengali, English and Italian, and edited The Cambridge Companion to Rabindranath Tagore (2020). He is an International Fellow of the British Academy, an Honorary Fellow of the Asiatic Society, Kolkata, and a former Fellow of All Souls College, Oxford, among other distinctions. Supriya Chaudhuri is Professor Emerita in the Department of English, Jadavpur University, India. She has held visiting appointments at the universities of Oxford, Cambridge and Paris-Sorbonne, among others. Her research interests include Shakespeare and Renaissance literature, Indian cultural history, modernism, theory, cinema, sport and urban studies. Among recent publications are her edited/co-edited books, Envisioning the Indian City: Spaces of Encounter (2025); Religion and the City in India (2022) and Commodities and Culture in the Colonial World (2018). Recent journal articles have appeared in Études Anglaises (2024), Spenser Studies (2024, 2023), BioScope (2023), Thesis 11 (2021), Postcolonial Studies (2021), and Literature Compass (2020). Published book-chapters include contributions to The Oxford Handbook of Modern Indian Literatures (2024), the Bloomsbury Handbook of Postcolonial Print Cultures (2023), The Form of Ideology and the Ideology of Form: Cold War, Decolonization and Third World Print Cultures (2022), Machiavelli Then and Now: History, Politics, Literature (2022), The Cambridge Companion to Tagore (2020), and The Cambridge History of Travel Writing (2019); translations appear in the Oxford Tagore Translations series and The Essential Tagore (Harvard UP). Rukmini Bhaya Nair is Professor (Hon..) of Linguistics and English at the India Institute of Technology Delhi (IITD). Currently Global Professorial Fellow at Queen Mary University of London (QMUL), she received her PhD from the University of Cambridge and was awarded an honorary doctorate by the University of Antwerp for her contributions to narrative theory. Author of 10 books and around 150 articles, her widely acclaimed academic books include: Lying on the Postcolonial Couch: The Idea of Indifference; Narrative Gravity: Conversation, Cognition, Culture; and Poetry in a Time of Terror: Essays in the Postcolonial Preternatural (Oxford University Press, 2002, 2003, 2009). Her most recent book is the reference volume (co-edited with Peter de Souza) Keywords for India: A Conceptual Lexicon for the 21st

Her most recent article is “Archewriting: The Symbolic Evolution of Script and Narrative” in The Oxford Handbook of Human Symbolic Evolution (2024). Nair’s research interests lie at the intersection of literary, linguistics and cognitive studies, including AI. Her work attempts to bring together what we know of the human mind in its evolutionary aspect and its everyday patterns of behaviour with what we know about current cultural patterns of social inclusion and exclusion on the Indian subcontinent and worldwide. Nair’s latest major grants have been from the Department of Science and Technology (DST) to conduct basic research on ‘Language, Culture and Emotion’ (2009-2013) under its Cognitive Sciences Initiative (CSI); and the Indian Council of Social Science Research (ICSSR) on ‘Cognitive Capabiilities in Education” (2014-2016). She was Indian Team Leader for the nine-country project on the “Geography of Philosophy” (2017-2023 https://www.geographyofphilosophy.com/teamleaders). She also writes widely on social issues in national and international popular print and electronic media, and has contributed regularly to forums such as Mark Tully’s BBC Program ‘Something Understood’ and ABC Australia’s ‘The Books Show’ as well as an NDTV Opinion Column, e.g. https://www.ndtv.com/opinion/my-encounterswith-stephen-hawking-in-delhi- 1823729. In addition, she is an award-winning poet who has published three volumes of poetry with Penguin: The Hyoid Bone, The Ayodhya Cantos and Yellow Hibiscus. The Oxford Companion to Modern Poetry (2014) writes that her work “widely admired… for its postmodern approach to lyrical meaning and feminine identity”. Her writings, creative and critical, have been included in the syllabi of Chicago, Delhi. Harvard, Kent, Toronto and other universities. Her poem ‘Gargi’s Silence’ is on the syllabus for all entry level Humanities and B. Com students at Delhi U. Three forthcoming books in 2025-2026 are: Deception: Mind, Metaphor, Memes and the Mimicry Machine; In the Name of Rushdie: Postcolonialism, Prophesy and the Platonic Censors and Postcolonial Pragmatics, as well as a book of poems titled Shataka: The Quarantined Tongue. Nair believes she does research in linguistics for the same reason that she writes poetry – to discover the limits and possibilities of language. Rita Kothari is Professor of English and the Head of the Department of English at Ashoka University. She earned her MPhil and her PhD in Literary Studies and Translation from Gujarat University, Ahmedabad. She has previously taught at St. Xavier’s College (Ahmedabad), MICA (Ahmedabad), and the Indian Institute of Technology, Gandhinagar. She is a multilingual scholar and translator whose work spans across different disciplines such as literature, cultural studies, anthropology, sociology, and history. Her questions emerge from observations of regions and communities in the western part of the Indian subcontinent—Gujarat, Kutch, and Sindh. Her ethnographic research on marginal communities—through religion, caste, occupation, and gender— focuses upon narratives of identity, raising questions of both linguistic and cultural translation. Professor Kothari has translated extensively from Gujarati and Sindhi into English and occasionally vice versa. Her translations, as well as her edited volumes, have made significant contributions to the field of language politics and translation. Her latest book, Uneasy Translations: Self, Experience and Indian Literature, interweaves her personal journey as an academic into reflections around self, language, and translation, with an eye upon the intangibly available category of experience. Her teaching interests include language politics, caste and communalism, Bollywood, Indian literature, translation studies, Partition, border studies, Gujarat studies, and Sindh studies. text: Her translations, as well as her edited volumes, have made significant contributions to the field of language politics and translation. Her latest book, Uneasy Translations: Self, Experience and Indian Literature, interweaves her personal journey as an academic into reflections around self, language, and translation, with an eye upon the intangibly available category of experience. Her teaching interests include language politics, caste and communalism, Bollywood, Indian literature, translation studies, Partition, border studies, Gujarat studies, and Sindh studies. Movements across languages, contexts, and cultures form the fulcrum of her interests, making translation the prism through which she sees the Indian context. Soujit Das is an independent researcher and curator working between Delhi– NCR and Kolkata. Trained at the Dept of Art History at Kala Bhavan, Visva- Bharati, Santiniketan, he further earned his PhD from the Department of Humanities and Social Sciences, IIT Roorkee, with a dissertation exploring Occidentalism in imperial Mughal paintings. He has previously been associated as a full-time faculty member at the Government College of Art and Craft Calcutta and Amity University, Noida, and also served as guest faculty at the College of Art, University of Delhi, and the Department of History, Lady Brabourne College, Kolkata. His research centres on cross- cultural artistic encounters in early-modern India. Current projects investigate the intersections of art history, death studies, and religious studies, that include a long-term documentation project of ‘Armenian Christian grave markers across India’ and ‘Imaging Death in Mughal Paintings’; he also works on the peripheral visual cultures of colonial Bengal. His recent scholarship has appeared in Indo-Asiatische Zeitschrift (Museum of Asian Art Berlin), Lalit Kala Akademi publications, Springer Singapore, Journal of East West Thought (CAL POLY), Take on Art, Art & Deal, Art East and several other platforms. Early 2026, he will be joining Museum Rietberg, Zurich, as the inaugural Goswami–Beach–Fischer (GBF) Foundation Post-Doctoral Research Fellow. **Overview of Bachelor of Science (BS) Program**

The Bachelor of Science (BS) in Behavioral Sciences and
Predictive Analytics (BSPA) is
inherently interdisciplinary, merging two distinct but complementary fields: Behavioral
sciences and predictive analytics. The program integrates the study of human Behavior
with advanced data analysis techniques, providing students with a holistic understanding
of both qualitative and quantitative dimensions of human actions and decisions. This
unique combination addresses the need for professionals who can interpret human Behavior
using data-driven approaches, making it relevant across various sectors such as
healthcare, public policy, marketing, and social sciences.

The program's interdisciplinary nature equips students
with the tools to analyze
complex social issues using Behavioral insights and predictive models. The dual focus on
theoretical understanding and practical application enables students to bridge the gap
between humanities and data science, preparing them for a wide range of careers where
both critical thinking and technical expertise are essential.

In summary, the curriculum draws from multiple academic
disciplines, ensuring that
graduates are well-rounded professionals capable of addressing complex societal
challenges through innovative, interdisciplinary methods.

**Core Training in Year 1 and Year 2:**

Strong Foundation with core courses in Behavioral Sciences, Neurosciences, Social
Sciences, Humanities, Economics, Mathematics, Computer Programming, Data Science,
Machine Learning, Psychology & Cognitive Sciences.

**Advanced Learning in Year 3 and Year
4**

Advanced and specialisation courses in Data Science, Machine Learning, Behavioural
Sciences, Business Strategy, Psychology, Economics, Humanities & Social Sciences,
and Policy Making Projects, Semester-long Industry Experience, Basket of Elective
Courses across the curriculum of IIT Jammu.

# Page Title: Humanities and Social Sciences | IIT Jammu

## Cyber Security and Privacy Awareness

**Date:**Thursday, November 21, 2024

The Department of Humanities and Social Sciences (HSS) at IIT Jammu successfully hosted a workshop as the second chapter of the ‘Manthan Series’, which focused on ‘**Cyber Security and Privacy Awareness.**’ Dr. Rajendra Kumar Solanki served as the keynote speaker for the event. An expert in information security, Dr. Solanki has made significant contributions to e-governance at IIT Jammu.

The workshop consisted of two sessions. The first session addressed security practices, such as password safety, while the second session discussed privacy measures related to social media. Dr. Amitash Ojha, the Head of the Department at HSS, concluded the session by emphasizing the importance of privacy in safeguarding consumer data from targeted marketing.

Helmed by Prof. Bijoy H. Boruah, Senior Faculty, HSS, the Department weekly gathers for seminar sessions on subjects and questions of “current importance and that of enduring interest” (Boruah, email). Since November 2021, Wednesday afternoons have been reserved for faculty and scholars to come together for discussions and deliberations that span the breadth of humanities and social sciences, including reflective understandings of contemporary technoscientific progress and its influence on humanity.

The broad format of these sessions provides the Speaker of the week with 35 to 45 mins of presentation time. The designated Principal Commentator responds to the presentation in 10 minutes, after which the presentation is open for all to have spirited discussions and critical insights that speculate, build on, and branch out, probing and crossing disciplinary boundaries. Apart from the Department’s faculty and research scholars, these discussions have frequently been led by distinguished academicians from and outside IIT Jammu.

# Page Title: Humanities and Social Sciences | IIT Jammu

## Empowering Tribal Women: A Comprehensive Analysis of Socio-Educational Status of Gujjar
and Bakerwal Women in Pir Panjal Range

Dr Sayantan Mandal, the co-principal investigator of the Tribal Project, along with Marve
B Sofi and Sayari Mishra (PhD scholars of HSS, IIT Jammu), were responsible to examine
and empirically understand the barriers to higher education and empowerment of Gujjar
and Bakerwal women residing in the Pir Panjal Range of Jammu and Kashmir. These women
encounter systemic discrimination, experience restricted access to education and
healthcare and are constrained by gender roles. The main aim of this study is to gain
insight into the socio-educational status and needs of these women while also
identifying the primary obstacles contributing to their social, educational, and
economic disadvantage. This study investigates the influence of cultural norms, gender
inequality, and socio-economic factors on individuals’ lives, specifically
examining issues such as restricted educational opportunities, limited access to
healthcare services, disparities in technology usage, reduced political engagement, and
diminished decision-making authority.

The study employed a mixed-method approach, incorporating interviews, focus groups,
observations, and surveys to comprehensively comprehend the social dynamics, cultural
practices, and gender roles within the project. The report presents a comprehensive set
of recommendations aimed at fostering sustainable and beneficial transformations within
the Gujjar and Bakerwal communities. The primary objective of this initiative is to
foster enduring transformation and make a valuable contribution to the broader academic
conversation surrounding the rights of tribal women. The achievements of the project are
anticipated to serve as a paradigm for forthcoming endeavours and foster broader
societal change towards the promotion of gender equality and inclusive development.

The faculty members (Dr Sayantan Mandal) and researchers (Marve B Sofi and Sayari Mishra)
at HSS, IIT Jammu, are committed to offering additional assistance in this matter when
required.

# Page Title: Humanities and Social Sciences | IIT Jammu

## Ethics of AI

This topical and crucial research project is funded by IIT Jammu, and its principal
investigator is Dr Joby Varghese. It started in April 2023 and has a three-year tenure
to understand the technological basis of ethics in AI.

This project recognises AI's wide and expanding impact. Therefore, it is committed to
taking plural perspectives in philosophy, computer science, cognitive science,
sociology, economics, law, and public policy to understand and analyse issues related to
AI systems and robotics. Since the philosophical literature is relatively thin on many
important topics regarding the ethics of AI and robotics, the project’s interests
span from establishing the preliminary and necessary clarity on key issues, such as bias
and opacity in the decisions of AI systems, to developing these debates further and with
rigour.

The most critical outcome of this project would be the framing of broad ethics and
principles for the design, development, and deployment of AI in India. While it will be
drawing on similar global initiatives, it will be grounded in the Indian legal and
regulatory context.

# Eye Tracking and Attention

# Page Title: Humanities and Social Sciences | IIT Jammu

## Eye
Tracking and Attention

**Date:**Saturday, November 16, 2024

The Department of Humanities and Social Sciences (HSS) at IIT Jammu, in collaboration with the Action Control and Cognition Lab at the University of Hyderabad, hosted the “**Eye Tracking and Attention**” workshop as the first chapter of the **Manthan Series** on **16 November 2024**. Around 250 participants gathered to discuss technology’s impact on human cognition.

Keynote speaker Dr. Ramesh Kumar Mishra highlighted eye tracking's roles in bilingualism and cognitive control. Sessions included insights from ACCL members on usability research and Dr. Garima Singh’s techniques for improving focus. The workshop concluded with Dr. Amitash Ojha discussing the implications of eye movements in understanding cognitive processes.

Dr. Ankita Yadav is an Assistant Professor of Law at Dr. RMLNLU. She completed her LL.B.(Hons.) from Banaras Hindu University in 2013 and her LL.M. from Dr. RMLNLU in 2015, and she was awarded a silver medal in her LL.M. #### Ankit Kathuria

Get to know the iMaSS lab, click the link for details: https://sites.google.com/view/imass

**About me**

Dr. Ankit Kathuria is working as an Associate Professor in the Department of Civil Engineering and Humanities and Social Sciences Department at IIT Jammu. Throughout his career, he has undertaken diverse roles, including his current position as Associate Dean for E-Governance System and his previous role as Head of the Department of Civil Engineering and Associate Dean (Infrastructure Planning and Management). He received the prestigious SERB-SIRE Fellowship in 2022, allowing him to contribute to significant research related to roundabout safety at the Future Mobility Group, Newcastle University, UK, for six months.

Dr. Kathuria did postdoctoral research at the Indian Institute of Technology in Bombay, India. He obtained his Ph.D. in Civil Engineering (Transportation Engineering) from the Indian Institute of Technology Roorkee in 2017. He received a Masters in Planning degree with specialization in Transport Planning from the School of Planning and Architecture, New Delhi in 2013 and, holds a Bachelor of Technology degree in Civil Engineering from Jaypee University, India.

In the realm of research, Dr. Kathuria has led various projects funded by esteemed organizations such as the Ministry of Road Transportation and Highways, Science and Engineering Research Board, National Highway Authority of India, Central Road Research Institute, Jammu Kashmir Science Technology and Innovation Council, and Indian Institute of Technology Jammu.

Dr. Kathuria's focus on research is driven by a genuine desire to find practical solutions to various transportation planning and engineering problems. His research encompasses a range of disciplines, including behavioral and psychological aspects of traffic, road safety, public transportation system design and planning, traffic management using BT and wifi sensors, social sciences, data mining, mathematical modeling, and flexible pavements (secondary research area). Presently, he leads six research projects as the principal investigator, aligning with his research interests.

In his mentoring capacity, Dr. Kathuria guides seven Ph.D. students, out of which one student has already submitted his thesis for further evaluation. He is grateful for the opportunities to contribute to academic discourse, with over 47 peer-reviewed research and conference papers to his name. Additionally, he is honored to serve as a reviewer for respected journals such as TR part A, TR part D, Accident Analysis and Prevention, Case Study on Transport Policy, Transport Policy, Transportation Letters and Journal of Transportation Engineering. Dr. Kathuria remains deeply committed to advancing knowledge and expertise in the humble pursuit of contributing to the field of transportation Planning and engineering.

##### Research Interest

**Primary research interest**

a) Behavioural and psychological aspects of drivers in heterogeneous disordered traffic conditions

b) Proactive approaches to road safety, including the use of surrogate safety measures

c) Planning and design of dedicated powered two-wheeler lanes for urban and non-urban highways

d) Simulation-based AV's and human-driven vehicle interaction

e) Public transportation system design and planning with primary focus on Bus Rapid Transit Systems

f) Exploring adoption intentions of e-shared mobility alternatives

g) Traffic management using BT and Wifi sensors

**Secondary research interest**

h) Utilization of surface-modified aggregates in asphalt mixes

- Garima Singh
- Assistant Professor

#### Garima Singh

##### Research Interest

Neural mechanisms of reward processing, decision-making and aberrant behaviours in clinical versus healthy populations through neuroimaging methods, delay discounting, metacognition, and memory dynamics.

##### Education Qualification

**PhD in Psychology,** University of New Mexico, USA (2019-2023)

**Master of Science in Psychology,**University of New Mexico, USA (2019-2021)

**Bachelor of Science in Psychology,** Idaho State University, USA (2016-2019)

##### Academic Interests

Dr. Garima Singh’s academic interests lie at the intersection of cognitive neuroscience and psychology, with a particular focus on the neural mechanisms underlying reward processing, decision-making, and aberrant behaviors in clinical versus healthy populations. Using advanced neuroimaging methods, including EEG, her work aims to deepen our understanding of how neural pathways impact cognitive and behavioral health, especially in relation to psychiatric conditions affecting the reward system.

In addition to these areas, Dr. Singh pursues separate research in delay discounting, metacognition, and memory dynamics, exploring how these processes influence decision-making and cognitive function across various contexts. Her broader research includes examining the role of stress, depression, and substance misuse on brain function, and investigating social factors such as memory distortion from social media and social influences on memory recall.

Dr. Singh’s research ultimately aims to develop effective diagnostic tools and contribute to a comprehensive understanding of cognitive health, advancing interventions for mental health challenges and aberrant behaviors.

##### Research Experience

1. Research Scholar, 2019-2023, University of New Mexico, USA

Area of work: Induced changes in the brain via affective imagery, decision-making dynamics, reward positivity, reward learning, and attentional bias in clinical populations.

2. Research Assistant, 2017-2019, Idaho State University, USA

Area of work: Sexual impulsivity and monetary delay discounting, Metacognition health and memory (perceptive memory, procedural memory, semantic memory).

#### Muhammed Haneefa A.P.

##### Research Interest

Social Stratification and Mobility; Sociology of Education; Climate Change and Environmental Marginalities; Human-Wildlife Conflict and Multispecies Relations (with emerging work on Anthrotherology); Sociology of Sports

##### Education Qualification

- Postdoctoral Fellow, Department of Humanities and Social Sciences, IIT Delhi, 2021-2024
- Ph.D. in Sociology, Jawaharlal Nehru University (JNU), New Delhi, Awarded 2019
- M.Phil. in Sociology, Jawaharlal Nehru University (JNU), New Delhi, Awarded 2013
- M.A. in Sociology, Jawaharlal Nehru University (JNU), New Delhi, 2010

##### Academic Interests

Introduction to Sociological Imagination; Ethnographic and Qualitative Research Methods; Environmental Sociology; Social Stratification; Foundational Thinkers in Social and Cultural Anthropology

# Page Title: Humanities and Social Sciences | IIT Jammu

## Gendered Belonging: Women and Work in Post-Partition Kolkata

**Date:**Friday, March 21, 2025

The Department of Humanities and Social Sciences at IIT Jammu organized a workshop as part of the **eighth chapter** of the 'Manthan Series,' titled “**Gendered Belonging: Women and Work in Post-Partition Kolkata**,” on **March 21, 2025**. The event featured Dr. Debjani Sengupta, an Associate Professor at the University of Delhi, as the resource person. The workshop aimed to explore the socio-economic and political challenges faced by refugee women in post-Partition Kolkata, emphasizing how displacement reshaped their roles in society.

The event began with a brief inauguration that set the stage for the discussions to follow. In the first session, Dr. Sengupta addressed the gendered impact of economic migration. She explained how women, previously confined to domestic roles, were compelled to seek employment in unfamiliar urban settings, competing with men in industries such as mills, factories, and offices. This shift, she argued, led to a redefinition of modernity for these women, challenging established societal norms.

Dr. Sengupta drew upon literary and cinematic representations to illustrate her points, discussing works like "Meghey Dhaka Tara" by Shaktipada Rajguru and "Bwadwip" by Sabitri Roy. These pieces vividly depict the struggles and resilience of refugee women in the post-Partition era. She emphasized how these narratives provide crucial insights into the lived experiences of displaced women, blending historical and moral dimensions with personal stories.

The second session featured an engaging interaction between Dr. Sengupta and the participants, allowing attendees to discuss the broader implications of gendered displacement. The discussion explored how refugee women not only redefined labor dynamics but also reshaped familial and social structures. Participants actively engaged in conversations about the intersections of gender, work, and migration, drawing parallels between historical displacement and contemporary issues faced by migrant women today.

The event successfully provided a platform for critical discussions on gender, displacement, and economic mobility, fostering a deeper understanding of the challenges faced by women in post-Partition Kolkata. Dr. Sengupta’s insights shed light on the resilience of these women, their struggles for survival, and their role in shaping the socio-economic fabric of the city. The session concluded with closing remarks, encouraging further academic inquiry into the themes discussed.

**Call for Papers

ICSSR-Sponsored National Conference

GST Reforms: Transforming India’s Economic Landscape**

**14 April 2026**

Organised by

**Department of Humanities and Social Sciences**

Indian Institute of Technology Jammu, Jammu

**About the conference**

The implementation of the Goods and Services Tax (GST) on 1 July 2017 marked a
transformative milestone in India’s fiscal history. Conceived under the
principle of “One Nation, One Tax,” GST sought to unify India’s
fragmented indirect tax system, eliminate cascading taxation, and create a seamless
national market.

Over the years, GST has evolved through structural reforms, technological
integration, and policy recalibrations. The recent rationalisation of tax
slabs—moving toward a simplified rate structure centred primarily on 5 percent
(merit rate) and 18 percent (standard rate), effective 22 September
2025—represents a significant step toward enhancing transparency, compliance,
and efficiency. The Input Tax Credit (ITC) mechanism has strengthened value-chain
neutrality, while the Composition Scheme has reduced compliance burdens for small
enterprises with turnover up to ₹1.5 crore.

Given the central role of GST in shaping India’s economic governance,
industrial competitiveness, and fiscal federalism, this ICSSR-sponsored national
conference aims to bring together scholars, policymakers, practitioners, and
researchers to critically examine the multifaceted impact of GST reforms. The
conference will particularly focus on implications for the MSME sector,
entrepreneurship, rural and urban livelihoods, and future reform trajectories.

**Conference Themes**

Submissions may address (but are not limited to) the following broad themes:

1. **GST and the MSME Sector**

- Compliance costs and formalisation
- Credit access and working capital dynamics
- Digital integration and tax administration
2. **Entrepreneurship and the Startup Ecosystem: Role of GST**

- Business climate and ease of doing business
- Innovation, competitiveness, and supply-chain integration
- Sectoral case studies
3. **Impact of GST on Rural and Urban Livelihoods**

- Employment and income effects
- Informal-to-formal transition
- Regional disparities
4. **Fiscal Federalism in the GST Era: Navigating Revenue Dynamics**

- Centre–State fiscal relations
- Revenue buoyancy and tax rationalisation
- Inter-state trade and market integration
5. **Reimagining GST: The Road Ahead**

- Rate rationalisation and structural reforms
- Digital governance and GSTN reforms
- International comparisons and policy lessons

**20 March 2026**to: hardeep.singh@iitjammu.ac.in

- Must include a 200-word abstract

All submissions should be original and unpublished work.

**Venue**

The Conference will be conducted in offline mode at the Indian Institute of
Technology Jammu.

**Important Dates**

## Heat Stress and Hospitalization in India

The 13th chapter of MANTHAN at IIT Jammu featured a compelling
talk on “Heat Stress and Hospitalization in India” by Abhishek Dureja from
Plaksha University.

The session shed light on the growing healthcare costs of
climate change, revealing that rising temperatures significantly increase
hospitalization rates—especially in nephrology and pediatrics—leading to
higher public healthcare expenditure. It emphasized the urgent need for
climate-responsive healthcare planning and policy interventions, particularly in
developing countries.

The discussion sparked thoughtful academic dialogue on the
intersection of climate change, public health, and healthcare systems, providing
valuable insights for researchers, students, and policymakers alike.

Together, we explore how science and research can guide
sustainable solutions for a climate-resilient future.

### Dr. Garima Singh

Head of Department

- hod.hss@iitjammu.ac.in

The Department of Humanities & Social Sciences, IIT Jammu, is committed to suturing
STEM (Sciences, Technology, Engineering, Math) into the fabric of Arts, Humanities and
Social Sciences. This endeavour is being realised through an enmeshed network of cross
and trans-disciplinary courses, projects, and collaborations. The department encompasses
the disciplines such as English, Economics, Sociology, Philosophy, Psychology, Cognitive
Science, Culture and Polity, and other such prospective subjects in the wide gamut of
Humanities and Social Sciences.

The mission and goals of the Humanities and Social Sciences are consistent with those of
IIT Jammu in that we foster innovative learning experiences that will enable our
students/graduates to develop broad, tolerant, and cosmopolitan perspectives so that
they will be better prepared to function in a diverse, demanding, and global society.

**Our Mission**

1. To train students to be highly competent and critically minded through teaching and
research programmes that are internationally competitive and locally relevant.
2. To educate students to embody holistic knowledge, inspire critical thinking, possess
effective oral and written communication skills, and become global citizens.
3. To secure a conducive educational environment with an atmosphere of collaborative
academic research that enables faculty and students to partake in the intellectual
life of their disciplines and contribute to the well-being of their communities and
regions.
4. To ensure, through our work performance and service, that humanistic and social
values are pursued to benefit the individual, the institute, and the community.

**Our Vision**

1. To become nationally recognised for excellence in both instruction and
research/scholarship - in which faculty members are well-rounded academics who take
equal pride in instructional, scholarly/research, and service activities.
2. To reflect the aspirations of the communities that we serve in our academic
offerings and research outputs.
3. To equip our students with the skills and knowledge necessary to excel in their
professional careers and other spheres of life to which they are expected to
contribute.
4. To create an active cross-disciplinary intellectual environment that promotes
creative interactions between faculty and students.

HSS Department at IIT Jammu has its roots firmly grounded in the soil of emancipatory
learning and is on its journey to reach out for the sun. It hopes to grow its budding
branches into a fruit-laden tree in the luminescence of the future.

## IIT Jammu Organizes a Field Visit to Jambu Zoo

Jammu, [16–2-2024] – The Indian Institute of Technology Jammu, in an
initiative by the Centre for Essential Skills and HSS, has conducted a comprehensive
field visit to Jambu Zoo for its students. This educational excursion, meticulously
organized by Dr. Quleen Kaur Bijral, Coordinator of Zuban-e-Kalam, HSS, and Dr. Amitash
Ojha from the Center for Essential Skills, is designed to furnish students with a
profound understanding of the delicate equilibrium that exists between flora, fauna, and
human societies. The initiative aims to foster an ecocritical appreciation of wildlife,
encouraging students to look beyond textbooks and understand the intricate connections
in natural habitats. This field visit is part of a broader educational endeavor that
includes heritage tours to both celebrated and lesser-known sites within Jammu and
Kashmir, promoting the region's rich cultural diversity.

Students gained firsthand experience of ecosystem dynamics, enhancing their understanding
of ecological balance and biodiversity. By seeing animals in real life, students develop
empathy towards living creatures and a sense of responsibility towards environmental
conservation and sustainable living practices.The trip offered a unique opportunity for
students to develop essential skills such as observation, analysis, and empathy towards
living organisms and their habitats. This initiative not only highlights IIT Jammu's
commitment to holistic education but also underscores the importance of integrating
practical experiences with academic learning.

The visit allowed students to make connections across various disciplines, including
biology, environmental science, geography, and even ethics. By engaging with the
environment around them, students are better prepared to become informed, responsible
citizens who value and work towards a sustainable future. The tour included discussions
on conservation efforts, the impact of human activities on wildlife, and the importance
of protected areas like zoos in preserving endangered species.

The event saw the coordinated efforts of Dr. Nilesh, Assistant Librarian, Mr. Vishal,
Student Welfare Office, alongside Sushmendera Kuril, Gaurav Jagarabad, and Subh Agarwal
of IIT Jammu, ensuring a seamless and enriching experience for the participants

## Integrating Teaching-Learning and Digital Education: A Study of Selected Higher
Education Institutions in Jammu & Kashmir and Ladakh

This project, headed by Dr Sayantan Mandal, is funded by IIT Jammu.

It is the first large-scale empirical longitudinal study conducted in multiple
institutions of Jammu & Kashmir and Ladakh UTs. It employs a mixed-methods approach
to investigate the integration of digital technologies with traditional teaching and
learning methods in Higher Education Institutions (HEIs) of these regions.

This project aims to identify empirically supported strategies for incorporating digital
technologies into the classroom and recommend evidence-based policy briefs.
Additionally, the project seeks to explore innovative teaching approaches and ensure
equitable access to high-quality higher education for all individuals, taking into
account the distinct characteristics of the region, as well as the socio-economic,
educational, and other diversities among learners and educators.

The primary aims of this study encompass familiarizing oneself with current teaching and
learning methodologies, examining obstacles related to academic and socio-economic
aspects, identifying regional and institutional factors that impact the incorporation of
online education alongside traditional classroom instruction, investigating disparities
and difficulties within and between institutions when integrating online education with
in-person teaching and offering evidence-based recommendations for suitable frameworks
that combine classroom and online teaching and learning in a context-specific manner.

The primary goal is to make a significant contribution to the growth of IIT Jammu as a
central institution in facilitating the integration of teaching, learning, and digital
technologies for higher education institutions in the northernmost region of India. This
will be achieved by enhancing teaching and learning through rigorous research and policy
interventions based on dynamic evidence.

### Labs

- Language Experiential Lab

### Language Experiential Lab

With an intent to reimagine the language learning process, a mobile (non-static)
Experiential Language Learning Lab has been newly developed at HSS, IIT Jammu. This
Experiential Language Lab, constituted in 2025, is a consolidation of various
methods and modes of Language instruction and training, supplemented by dedicated
language learning software systems. The Lab then, is primarily a system of learning
that is highly adaptable to different teaching spaces and classrooms, without being
fettered to contingent infrastructural conditions.

At HSS IIT Jammu, we also have a dedicated space– a Multipurpose Hall to refine
the academic English and interpersonal Communication skills of the students. The
English language proficiency course offered by the HSS for the new cohort of BTech
students would enable them to excel in all the dimensions for developing the
essential skills i.e. LSRW with a student-centered approach.

Apart from the dedicated software systems, we guide students in their learning
journey through Open-Access digital platforms and applications, operated through
spaces with projecting facilities and AV equipment, as well as outdoor spaces, to
encourage creative productions and disseminations, recitations and performances by
the students, within a moderated participatory environment.

The Lab caters to the level based needs of the learners by providing them with an
immersive experience, with AI tools and software and a variety of interactive
activities led by the Instructors.

The Hall is a forum for the confluence of numerous ideas and thoughts with Debate,
Film and Reading Clubs catering to the spirited target group and indulging in
stimulating discussions.

The Language Experiential Lab is being implemented along with classroom engagement
features and dedicated AI enhanced software to impart self paced learning modules.
The software consisting of levels mapped with CEFR guidelines issues certifications
to the students that they can link with their professional achievements.

The vision of this lab ensures that students actively participate and hone their soft
skills. The language proficiency course offered here is a dedicated credit course to
set the foundation for their future career paths.

# Page Title: Humanities and Social Sciences | IIT Jammu

## Locating Feminist Counterpublics in 19th Century Bengal

Led by Dr Sukanya Mondal, the project is funded by IIT Jammu and is scheduled to be
completed by 2027.

The aim of this project is to examine the changes which the women's quarter in 19th
Century Bengal underwent as an effect of the rapidly evolving public sphere, literacy
drive, and various other social-political reformations. The so-called public sphere
(public meetings, lectures, etc., even the parlours of well-to-do families) was almost
men-only spaces.

Therefore, this project is trying to locate the spaces or situations when women met to
exchange ideas related to contemporary society, politics, etc. The theoretical point of
departure for this study is Nancy Fraser's critique of Jurgen Habermas' idea of
the Public Sphere.

The Department of Humanities and Social Sciences, Indian Institute of Technology Jammu, is organising a one-day interdisciplinary workshop titled Reimagining Tribal Studies on 13 January, from 9:30 AM to 5:30 PM, at Sanskriti, Mansar. The inaugural session, scheduled between 10:30 AM and 12:30 PM, will be honoured by the presence of the Hon’ble Minister, Mr. Javed Ahmed Rana, as the Chief Guest, and Dr. Javaid Rahi, noted scholar and cultural practitioner, as the Guest of Honour.

The workshop is conceived as a platform to critically engage with the histories, cultures, knowledge systems, and contemporary realities of tribal communities in India, with a special focus on the Gujjar and Bakerwal communities of Jammu and Kashmir. It seeks to move beyond static and stereotypical representations of tribal life and instead foreground lived experiences, narrative traditions, indigenous knowledge practices, and evolving social formations.

Bringing together more than 300 participants, including students from Gujjar and Bakerwal hostels, faculty members, researchers, policymakers, and invited experts, the workshop aims to encourage interdisciplinary dialogue across the humanities, social sciences, and allied fields. Key themes include cultural heritage and memory, oral and narrative traditions, identity and mobility, ethical community engagement, and the possibilities and challenges of digital documentation and preservation.

Through keynote addresses, panel discussions, and interactive sessions, the workshop aspires to create a reflective academic space that is both ethically grounded and socially responsive. It also seeks to strengthen collaboration between academic institutions, community stakeholders, and policymakers, contributing to more inclusive and context-sensitive approaches to tribal studies.

# Page Title: Humanities and Social Sciences | IIT Jammu

## Marketing Analytics: Unlocking Business Performance Through Data-Driven Insights

**Date:**Wednesday, April 16, 2025

The Department of Humanities and Social Sciences (HSS) at IIT Jammu hosted a Research
Talk on **April 16, 2025**, as a **ninth chapter** of the
‘**Manthan Series**’. The talk focused on the areas of
‘**Marketing Analytics**’ and how business performances could
be enhanced through data analytics driven insights.

The talk traced the history of marketing and how attitudinal and behavioural studies
surrounding it have drastically changed since the adoption of technology and artificial
intelligence systems.

Prof. Marcos Severo from the Federal University of Goiás (Brazil) discussed case
studies from Brazil which show the multiple methods of data collection that marketing
analytics relies on and the role of artificial intelligence in advancements like real
time individualised strategies for developments in sales revenue and overall business
performance enhancements. He traced the changing landscape of data collection sources
and the different models of knowledge systems which direct the organization of these
data sets. The talk highlighted how marketing has made a transition and evolved from an
intuition to an evidence era relying on data and analysis.

The talk provided valuable insights to the participants through a combination of
theoretical frameworks and specific case studies to understand the relationship between
psychology, marketing, economics and technology.

# Page Title: Humanities and Social Sciences | IIT Jammu

## Mind and Ethics

**HSS Organises 3 days Workshop on Mind and Ethics**

Dept. of Humanities and Social Sciences, Indian Institute of Technology Jammu organized a
3 - days Workshop on “ Mind and Ethics”” for the students of the
institute. The program was curated to foster understanding of Mental health and Ethics
among the students keeping in view the emerging rate of mental health problems in IITs .

The program was coordinated by Dr. Suchismita Roy, Visiting Faculty, Sociology, HSS. In
the welcome address, Dr. Roy highlighted the schedule with emphasis on the relevance as
well as the proposed goal of the workshop. Goals of the workshop were to articulate
rationales for integrating ethics in participants, professional and social life - what
to consider before making decisions and principles of justice, how to handle stress,
anxiety, substance abuse and how to be empathetic, introduce mental health awareness
while addressing the stigma on mental health issues and awareness about relevant social
legislation related to confidentiality, plagiarism, ethical consent, women cell.

On the First day, Dr. Abhiruchi Chatterjee, the eminent community psychiatrist, initiated
the workshop with an interactive session discussing myths around mental health and how
to handle stress.The second day, Mr. SS Sharma, Asst Professor of SMVDU, focused on how
to handle ethical dilemmas with a crisp introduction on ethics and critical thinking.

Prof Renuka Devi, IIM Jammu, took a session on ethics in the organisation. In this
session, students learned different concepts of business ethics ,do’s and
don'ts in the organisation. She also discussed Indian perspective of ethics. On the
last day Dr. Bhaskar Mukherjee, psychiatrist and neuroscientist explored the
relationship between neuro-science and engineering , and how the brain works and recent
developments in brain research.

The concluding session of the workshop was conducted by Advocate Shakeel Ahmed, who
introduced relevant social legislation to the student in simple language. He started his
session with the quotes of the famous poet Ghalib.

Workshop Sessions were chaired by Dr. Gaurav A Bhaduri, Dr. Bhumika R, Ms Ishita Mahajan,
Dr. Sukanya Mandal and Dr Quleen Kaur Bijral, H.o.D, HSS. The speakers were presented
with mementos. More than 85 % of participants said that they would like to recommend
this course to others. Students felt this course highlighted some important aspects
which they don't learn in regular classes of academics. Students appreciated new
concepts, open discussion about mental health issues , friendly interaction,real life
examples and cases used in the workshop. The event was concluded with vote of thanks by
Dr Suchismita Roy. Special thanks to Psychologist Ms Akansha Joshi, Dr. Joby, and Mr
Shalu, Mr Abhishek Caretaker, Mr Sunil Jamwal.

**Date:**Monday, December 2, 2019

# Page Title: Humanities and Social Sciences | IIT Jammu

## 3-Day National Conference

**HSS IIT Jammu Conducts
3-Day National Conference**

HSS IIT Jammu organized a 3-day National Conference on “Conflict and the
Everyday: Narratives from the Personal and Public Realms” from 21st- 23rd
February 2020.

Prof
Shiv VisvanathanProfessor & Vice Dean and Executive Director, Centre for the
Study of Science, Society, and Sustainability, OP Jindal Global University,
Sonepat was the Keynote Speaker of the conference. The plenary session of the
conference had eminent speakers Dr KB Veio Pou, Prof. Meera Velayudhan,Dr.
Amitabh Vikram Dwivedi, Adhik Kadam, who disseminated their knowledge to the
participants for a better understanding of the notions of conflict and the
everyday.

Dr.
Amitash Ojha, H.O.D. HSS, welcomed the esteemed dignitaries, faculties,
participants while briefing on the significance of the Conference.

Dr. Quleen Kaur Bijral, Convener, addressed the attempt of re-thinking of the
term ‘Conflict’ by listening to the ‘insignificant’ and
‘everyday’ narratives.

Dr. Bhumika, co-convener, gave the vote of thanks while expressing gratitude
towards -

Director, IIT Jammu, Prof. Manoj Singh Gaur,

Dean, Academic Affairs, Prof. Ashoke Kumar Sarkar

Dean, Continuing Education and Outreach, Prof. Ashok Kumar Ahuja

Dean, Student Welfare, Prof. Chandan Gosh

Div. Chairperson, Prof. Uma Shankar

OSD, Cmde Naresh Kumar and other eminent members of the institute and outside.

On the
second day, there was a theatrical performance by Natrang, which is a Theatre
& Performing Arts Institution of Jammu. Under the direction Shri Balwant
Thakur, Padmashree awarded theatre director, two plays or Nukkad Drama were
enacted:

'Girgit' in Hindi based
on a Russian short story by Anton Chekhov. A hard-hitting satire on the present
socio-political system.

'Gatt' a multilingual play based on an Urdu story by Kishan Chander
called ‘Khadda’. The play reflects the plight of the poor masses who
continue to suffer for want of help from those at the helm of affairs.

The
third day concluded with a valedictory session wherein Prof. Ashoke Sarkar
distributed the best paper presenter award. The session was anchored by Dr.
Suchismita Roy, co-convener of the conference in coordination with Dr. Sayantan
Mandal.

February 21, 2020

# Page Title: Humanities and Social Sciences | IIT Jammu

### HSS Courses

- PhD Programme

### PhD Programme

The Humanities and Social Sciences Department, at IIT Jammu offers a five year PhD programme. Students applying for this Programme must have cleared the National Eligibility Test (NET) in the relevant subject. The Department seeks highly motivated and enthusiastic scholars willing to inculcate a robust research methodology and contribute to the Department’s fields of research

The potential topics for PhD specializations offered by the department are Cognitive Science, Neuroscience, Experimental Psychology - strong programming skills are required, preferably in MATLAB or Python; Tribal Literature, Subaltern Studies; Ethics, Philosophy of Science, Western Epistemology; Industrial and Financial economics, Agricultural Economics, Development Economics, Financial, Environmental and Institutional Economics- Expected to have basic understanding of Mathematics and Statistics; Applied Psychology/ Psychology/ Social Psychology/ Gender, Familiarity with Quantitative/Qualitative data analysis, SPSS.

Please refer to the institute website for the eligibility criteria in the specific domain of specialization.

# Page Title: Humanities and Social Sciences | IIT Jammu

## Policy Brief 1 on Six Districts of J&K | March 2024.

1. The policy brief is prepared by Dr. Sayantan Mandal and Sheriya Sareen, Department of Humanities and Social Sciences (HSS), Indian Institute of Technology Jammu (IIT Jammu), J&K.
2. Policy brief 1 is primarily based on the research project Integrating Teaching and Learning and Digital Education (ITLDE), a large-scale, empirical, multi institutional, longitudinal study, funded by IIT Jammu. The data was collected in two phases employing questionnaire-based surveys of more than 4700 students and 370 teachers, 100 focus group discussions with students, 100 interviews with teachers, 25 interviews with administrators, and 50 classroom observations in 25 higher education institutions across six districts of Jammu & Kashmir.
3. Policy Brief 1 consists of evidence-based policy recommendations derived from the large-scale empirical study titled ‘Integrating Teaching Learning and Digital Education’ (ITLDE) for the Higher Education Institutions of J&K.
4. The purpose of this policy brief is to inform the concerned Ministries, Policymakers, University Administrators, and Faculty members about the evidence-based, empirically grounded policy recommendations to integrate digital education with teaching and learning practices in blended spaces.

- **Download Policy Brief**

# Page Title: Humanities and Social Sciences | IIT Jammu

## Possible Ethnographies of ‘Science and Religion’: An Exercise in Anthropology of the
Moderns

On April 13, 2026, at 2:00 PM, the Department of Humanities and Social Sciences at the
Indian Institute of Technology (IIT) Jammu hosted a talk titled “Possible
Ethnographies of ‘Science and Religion’: An Exercise in Anthropology of the
Moderns” as part of the Manthan Series (Chapter 14).

Led by Dr. Renny Thomas from the Indian Institute of Science Education and Research,
Bhopal, the session examined the relationship between science and religion through an
anthropological lens. Dr. Thomas highlighted how modern societies navigate knowledge
systems and belief structures. The talk encouraged interdisciplinary dialogue and
critical reflection on contemporary understandings of these topics.

The event attracted students, faculty, and researchers, promoting meaningful discussions
and academic engagement within the Manthan Series.

# Page Title: Humanities and Social Sciences | IIT Jammu

## Possible Ethnographies of Science and Religion: An Exercise in the Anthropology of the
Moderns

The Department of HSS is organizing an insightful talk as the
14th chapter of the MANTHAN series titled "**Possible Ethnographies of
Science and Religion: An Exercise in the Anthropology of the Moderns**."
The talk will be delivered by Dr. Renny Thomas from IISER Bhopal on **April 13,
2026, at 2 PM**.

Dive into the fascinating intersection of science and religion
as Renny shares his ethnographic experiences studying Indian scientists and their unique
religious-scientific worlds. Discover how anthropology can illuminate the negotiations
and collaborations between these domains, and gain new perspectives on science and
religion beyond the Western context.

Don’t miss out on this opportunity to engage in a
thought-provoking discussion!

# Page Title: Humanities and Social Sciences | IIT Jammu

- **Speaker**: Ms. Saloni Walia
- **Designation**: Ph.D. Scholar
- **Department**: Humanities and Social Sciences
- **Date, Day and Time**: 7th July, 2025, Monday, 3 p.m. onwards
- **Event**: Pre-Ph.D. Seminar
- **Venue**: North Block, Room 209

**Description**: The Pre-Ph.D. Seminar discussed
the doctoral thesis titled Beyond the Binary, Across the Spectrum: A Multimodal Reading
of Transgender Indian Narratives that engages with oral, textual, and visual narratives
to examine the diverse expressions of transgender identities in India. Positioned within
India’s socio-cultural and linguistic contexts, the study interrogates binary
gender norms and highlights how marginalized voices articulate resistance and
self-definition.

The research explores podcasts, literature, cinema, webcomics,
and photo-textual art to trace how questions of gender intersect with caste, class, and
region. It underscores the resilience and creativity of transgender individuals in
reimagining identity and generating inclusive cultural discourses.

Bringing together perspectives from literature, media, and
queer/trans studies, this interdisciplinary work contributes to widening the field of
transgender scholarship in India while advocating pathways toward a more equitable and
inclusive society.

## Smart Skilling: Experimental Evidence on Vocational Training Design

The 12th chapter of MANTHAN, saw an inspiring session by Dr.
Tanu Gupta (University of Southampton, Delhi) on “Smart Skilling: Experimental
Evidence on Vocational Training Design”.

The talk highlighted how blending sector-specific vocational
skills with project-based experiential learning and digital
competencies—especially social media for business—can significantly improve
women’s:

* Occupational alignment

* Self-employment participation

* Earnings and job satisfaction

The session sparked engaging discussions on evidence-based
skilling strategies and their policy relevance, showcasing how thoughtfully designed
training programs can empower women economically and strengthen India’s inclusive
workforce.

# Page Title: Humanities and Social Sciences | IIT Jammu

## Social Audit of P M Poshan Scheme

## Meenakshi Rajeev , Shafkat Dar and Karan Nathwani

India has implemented various nutrition-centric and nutrition-sensitive programs to
address malnutrition and promote better health outcomes. Pradhan Mantri Poshan Shakti
Nirman (PM POSHAN) Scheme is one of the foremost rights-based Centrally Sponsored
Schemes implemented in partnership with the States and UTs for providing one hot cooked
and nutritious meal to all children up to class VIII of Government and Government-aided
schools. The PM Poshan Shakti Nirman (PM POSHAN) Scheme, earlier known as the ‘National
Programme for Mid-Day Meal in Schools’ Scheme, was introduced in 2021. The UT of J & K
also adopted this scheme and reaped the benefits of the scheme. To understand the
functioning of the scheme in the entire ten districts of Jammu region, IIT Jammu, HSS
Department, has undertaken a social audit study led by Prof. Meenakshi Rajeev , Dr
Shafkat Dar and Dr Karan Nathwani with the support of the Directorate of PM Poshan of
the UT. The study covers all the ten districts of the Jammu Province. A Social Audit is
a systematic process through which the implementation, performance, and outcomes of a
public programme or scheme are examined with the active involvement of stakeholders.

# Page Title: Humanities and Social Sciences | IIT Jammu

## Social Shaping of Technology

**Date:**Wednesday, March 19, 2025

The Department of Humanities and Social Sciences (HSS) at IIT Jammu successfully hosted a workshop on **March 19, 2025**, as part of the **seventh chapter** of the ‘**Manthan Series**.’ The workshop focused on the theme of '**Social Shaping of Technology**.'

The event explored the interplay between technology and society, challenging the notion that technology develops independently and subsequently molds societal structures.

Dr. Sambit Mallick from the HSS department at IIT Guwahati discussed the influence of economic, political, and cultural factors on technological innovation. He highlighted how decisions made in technology development can impact various social groups and addressed the limitations of technological determinism. He also provided examples illustrating how societal dynamics shape technological advancements.

The workshop offered valuable insights into the relationship between technology and society, helping participants understand how broader social factors influence innovation.

# Page Title: Humanities and Social Sciences | IIT Jammu

## The Spectacle of AI Technology: Reflections on Ethics and Human Agency

**Date:**Friday, February 21, 2025

On February 21, 2025, Dept. of HSS, IIT Jammu proudly hosted the fifth chapter of Manthan Series as an impactful symposium '**Spectacle of AI Technology: Reflections on Ethics and Human Agency**', bringing together prominent scholars to delve into the crucial ethical and human-centric aspects of artificial intelligence (AI).

The event showcased five compelling sessions that addressed vital issues:

1. Dr. Amitash Ojha, HoD of the HSS department, shed light on the profound influence of AI on user decision-making and personal autonomy, prompting attendees to reflect on their agency in an AI-driven world.

2. Dr. Sushruth Ravish, Assistant Professor from HSS dept, IIT Kanpur, uncovered the delicate balance between transparency and functionality in explainable AI, highlighting the need for prudent approaches in trust-building with technology.

3. Dr. Joby Verghese, Assistant Professor from HSS dept, IIT Jammu confronted the ethical challenges in healthcare associated with AI, emphasizing the importance of informed consent and shedding light on the complexities of AI's decision-making processes.

4. Dr. Sreekumar Jayadevan, Assistant Professor from HSS dept, IIT Ropar, provocatively discussed the nature of responsibility in our interactions with AI, compelling participants to ponder the implications for human relationships and moral accountability.

5. Dr. Bijoy H. Boruah, Professor from HSS dept, IIT Jammu discussed the cultural narratives surrounding AI, questioning its portrayal as an omnipotent force and advocating for a reassessment of human control in the technological landscape.

The symposium concluded with a thought-provoking valedictory session and networking opportunities, leaving participants with valuable insights and a renewed commitment to engage with the ethical dimensions of AI. This event served as a vital platform for fostering dialogue and interdisciplinary collaboration to address the societal impact of AI, urging us all to navigate the future of technology with caution and conscience.

Empowering Tribal Women by Dr Sayantan Mandal

## Tribal Project or A Literary, Socio-Cultural, and Linguistic Study of Tribal World:
Setting up Gujjar & Bakerwal Repository

The Tribal Project between the HSS Department, IIT Jammu, and the Tribal Research
Institute intended to collect, interpret, and disseminate Gojri Folklore, understand the
worldview of Gojri through their language, and study women’s socio-educational
status in the life of Gujjars and Bakarwals living in the Pir-Panjal regions of the
Union Territory of Jammu and Kashmir. The focus was on tangible deliverables such as
digital repositories, reports, websites, and workshops.

On this note, an MOU was signed on 26 June 2022 between IIT Jammu and the Tribal Research
Institute, J&K, in the presence of the Hon’ble Lieutenant Governor, Manoj
Sinha, to establish a Tribal Chair in the institute. Director, IIT Jammu, Prof Manoj
Singh Gaur, announced the set up of a Tribal Chair at the institute. The objectives of
the Tribal Chair were to collect, transcreate, digitize, and disseminate the tribal
culture of Gujjars and Bakerwals. The PIs of the Tribal Project are: Dr Quleen Kaur
Bijral (PI), Dr Amitash Ojha (Co-PI) and Dr Sayantan Mandal (Co-PI).

Marve and Sayari, research scholars of the HSS Department, have been an integral part of
the work.
# Page Title: Humanities and Social Sciences | IIT Jammu

## 3-Day Tribal Workshop Organised by IIT Jammu and TRI, J&K

The Department of Humanities and Social Sciences (HSS), IIT Jammu, in collaboration with
the Tribal Research Institute (TRI), J&K, organized a three-day workshop on
“Tribal Praxis: Understanding Literary, Linguistic and Socio-cultural practices of
the tribal communities of J&K” from 4th-6th July, 2022. The main objective of
the workshop was to provide a platform for assimilation of ideas, theory and practice
while developing a roadmap for fruitful collaboration between IIT Jammu and TRI,
J&K. The Inaugural session to the three day workshop deliberated on the action
points of the MoU between IIT Jammu and TRI, J&K, Tribal chair, and the targets and
deliverables of the Tribal Project.

The welcome address to the inaugural session was delivered by Dr Amitash Ojha, Head of
the Department (HSS, IIT Jammu), wherein he highlighted the importance of the
collaboration between IIT Jammu, and TRI, J&K and the establishment of a Tribal
Chair with the goals of comprehensively gathering tribal data, transcreating,
digitizing, disseminating, and studying the tribal culture from a research-oriented
perspective through workshops, conferences, field trips, seminars, cultural programmes,
and exhibitions.

Dr Quleen Kaur Bijral, Assistant Professor & FIC, Oral Narrative Center
(Zuban-e-Kalam) HSS, IIT jammu, elucidated the theme of the workshop as how to achieve
its targets, and provide tangible and satisfactory outcomes. Dr Quleen highlighted that
before the initiation of any project, planning should be of primary focus. This is
wherein the hindsight advantage of experience of the session experts can be an
eye-opener to foster an interactive confluence of ideas enabling one to learn, reinforce
and find the best course of action.

Professor Manoj Singh Gaur, Director, IIT Jammu talked about the vision of IIT Jammu in
trend-setting research and the significance of the MoU with the Tribal Research
Institute in taking it a step further. He further emphasized that IIT Jammu's
mission has been to meet the diverse demands of various sectors from entrepreneurship,
cultural diversity, artisanship, arts and craft of J&K, including notably the young
brigade of students from various locales of the region and to fulfill the responsibility
to develop strategies to strengthen the regional diversity of J&K.

Dr Javaid Rahi, Chief Editor, J&K Academy of Art and Culture, painted a vibrant
picture of the culture and essence of the indigenous tribes of Jammu and Kashmir through
his words. He shared his rich experience of working with the two prominent tribal
communities of Gujjars and Bakerwals of J&K and the uniqueness of their
Socio-economic structure.

Dr Abdul Khabir, Deputy Director, Tribal Affairs Department and Nodal Officer of the
Tribal Research Institute eloquently summarized the very heart of this collaboration in
the sense that both IIT Jammu and TRI are premier institutions and share the same spirit
of research and service. Dr Khabir emphasized that the faculty and research scholars
from IIT Jammu, and research fellows of TRI, J&K comprises a team better equipped to
re-discover regional tales, songs, music and indigenous knowledge, and subsequently
creating opportunities for techno-enabled societal interventions.

The theoretical framework interspersing the theme of the workshop consisted of Literary
study of tribal cultures, logistical analysis of tribal language, and socio-cultural
understanding of tribal populations as well as policies and initiatives targeted for
their holistic development. Tangible outcomes of this collaboration between IIT Jammu
and TRI, J&K can be perceived in the form of collection, transcreation, and
dissemination of data, and consequently, development of a website, an extensive
repository, and more such research panels and workshops in the future.

**Expert Sessions: Brief Mentions**

Before the initiation of any project, planning should be of primary focus. This is
wherein the hindsight advantage of experience can be an eye-opener on errors, caution
and techniques, etc., further encouraging an interactive confluence of ideas enabling
one to learn, reinforce and find the best course of action. It also allows avenues for
peer review and constructive criticism.

The Inaugural session to the three day workshop deliberated on the action points of the
MoU between IIT Jammu and TRI, J&K, Tribal chair, and the targets and deliverables
of the Tribal Project. The theoretical framework interspersing the theme of the workshop
consisted of Literary study of tribal cultures, logistical analysis of tribal language,
and socio-cultural understanding of tribal populations as well as policies and
initiatives targeted for their holistic development. Tangible outcomes of this
collaboration can be perceived in the form of collection, transcreation, and
dissemination of data, and consequently, development of a website, an extensive
repository, and more such research panels and workshops in the future.

The faculty and research scholars from IIT Jammu, and research fellows of TRI, J&K
comprises a team better equipped to re-discover regional tales, songs, music and
indigenous knowledge, and subsequently creating opportunities for techno-enabled
societal interventions. Herein, the enthusiastic Btech students of IIT Jammu are an
asset in brainstorming and developing technological solutions to social problems. From a
broader perspective, this three-day workshop is an initiation to accepting, appreciating
and achieving cultural diversity and tolerance, getting out of echo-chambers, and
strengthening amical bonds of cultural bonhomie.

Professor Manoj Aggarwal shared his extensive experience and knowledge about the tribes
of Andaman and Nicobar Islands, as well as the challenges that he and his team faced
during his stay there. This facilitated a cognizance of the bottlenecks associated with
studying tribal populations and ways to navigate the same.

Professor Bipin Indurkhya shared his expertise on General Research Methodology,
guidelines and tools, with a specific focus on Qualitative research. His insight on
complex situations wherein there are many themes that are interlinked and cannot be
considered in isolation if one is to develop a comprehensive picture of the community in
question, by taking a bird’s eye view as well as a focused perspective
simultaneously is essential to streamlining studies on tribal populations.

Professor Bijoy Boruah discussed the philosophy of tribal ethos, culture diversity and
language richness. It led to a very fruitful dialogue between the participants regarding
the implications of the word ‘tribe’, identity and marginalization.

Professor Uma Shankar delivered an insightful and thought-provoking session on research
ethics and the essence of integrity. The crux of his session can be eloquently
summarized as ‘a researcher needs to be morally right and not politically
right’.

Dr Amitash Ojha elucidated metaphors, and its importance in the context of tribal
narratives. His session was a valuable insight on language and its essentiality in
various contexts. This discussion was further enunciated by Dr Apurva from IIM Jammu,
who discussed complex theories of linguistic determinism and linguistic relativity. Such
concepts are extremely relevant to understanding tribal narratives and folklores.

Ms Farhat Choudhary who has worked with Bakerwal women has extensive knowledge of their
cultural and socio-economic issues. We also had Mr Gorkh Nath, an artist and
representative of Gaddi tribes, who divulged into the essence of the art and culture of
the Gaddi tribes.

## HSS Organises 2-Day Research Writing Workshop

“No one ever told us these things about writing - we wish we had attended something
like this earlier as well!” - Participants, Academic Writing Workshop 2020.

IIT Jammu successfully hosted a two-day Academic Writing Workshop,
“**Work-in-Progress: Removing Roadblocks from Research
Writing**” organized by the Department of Humanities and Social
Sciences on 10th-11th January, 2020. The workshop consisted of hands-on writing and
reviewing sessions conducted by Dr. Anannya Dasgupta and Mr. Sameer Thomas from the
Center for Writing and Pedagogy, Krea University, Andhra Pradesh.

Each session was focused on generating writing output and the participants received
detailed feedback on their work. The workshop also discussed the nuances of structuring
the process of writing and thinking. The workshop was about strategizing research
techniques in order to generate a strong argument. This involved a step-by-step
engagement with the mechanics of constructing thoughts, ideas into sentences and
building a lucid and well-structured piece of research writing.

The workshop received a great response from all the 25 participants who came from the
following institutes:

1. IIT Roorkee
2. Shri Mata Vaishno Devi University
3. Central University of Haryana
4. Central University of Jammu
5. Lovely Professional University
6. Baba Ghulam Shah Badshah University
7. The University of Jammu
8. Central University of Himachal Pradesh
9. IIT Jammu

All the participants unanimously appreciated the relevance and the significance of the
topics discussed throughout the workshop.

## Introductory Workshop on Systematic Reviews

**Date:**Wednesday, January 21, 2026

The Department of Humanities & Social Sciences at IIT
Jammu successfully hosted a **Two-Day Introductory Workshop on Systematic Reviews,**as part of the **MANTHAN series (Chapter 11)**, on **January
21–22, 2026**, at the Jagti Campus.

Led by **Dr. Prachi Pundir from The George Institute for
Global Health, New Delhi,** the workshop offered participants a comprehensive
journey through the systematic review process.

Day 1 focused on foundational concepts, helping attendees
distinguish between systematic, narrative, and other review types, formulate precise
review questions, and develop protocols with hands-on database search exercises.

Day 2 emphasized the analytical and interpretative stages,
including screening, data management, risk of bias assessment, narrative synthesis, and
meta-analysis, reinforced through interactive group activities and practical Excel
exercises.

The workshop concluded with discussions on project management,
ethical considerations, team coordination, and next steps, equipping participants with
actionable insights to conduct high-quality systematic reviews and strengthen
evidence-based research skills.
